As AI agents, including large language models (LLMs), become embedded in daily life, they increasingly participate in such interactions and reshape social interaction structures. Yet they often fail to coordinate with humans in an effective, considerate, and natural manner. We hypothesize that this gap arises because existing approaches align model behavior with human demonstrations without explicitly quantifying the underlying norms that generate such behavior. We selected pedestrian-vehicle interaction as a representative dynamic interaction and developed a simplified experimental platform that captures its key interactive features. From 3,456 dynamic human interactions collected via this platform, we identified three principles underlying human social norms: outcome predictability, value alignment, and advantage awareness. Incorporating these principles into AI agents significantly improves human-AI coordination. In the closed-loop interaction task with humans, the social-norm-informed LLM achieved a nearly fourfold higher total score than the baseline strategy and outperformed human-human interactions by 43%. These findings indicate that formalizing tacit social norms into explicit, quantifiable principles can enable AI agents to achieve mutually beneficial coordination in dynamic interactions, supporting their more natural integration into human society.

AI.tldr: Formalizing implicit social norms into quantifiable principles enables AI agents to achieve dramatically better coordination with humans in dynamic interactions, nearly 4x better than baseline strategies.
AI.motivation: AI agents often fail to coordinate effectively with humans in dynamic interactions despite being increasingly embedded in daily life, creating a need to bridge the gap between human social norms and AI behavior.
AI.method: Studied pedestrian-vehicle interactions as a representative dynamic interaction, collected 3,456 human interactions via an experimental platform, identified three principles underlying social norms (outcome predictability, value alignment, advantage awareness), and incorporated these into AI agents.
AI.result: Social-norm-informed LLM achieved nearly fourfold higher total score than baseline strategy and outperformed human-human interactions by 43% in closed-loop interaction tasks with humans.
AI.conclusion: Formalizing tacit social norms into explicit, quantifiable principles enables AI agents to achieve mutually beneficial coordination in dynamic interactions, supporting more natural integration into human society.

title: LLM-Guided Task-Semantic Field Factorization for Industrial Process Forecasting
summary: Process industries rely on time-series forecasting and soft sensing to estimate quality variables that are hard to measure online. Labeled data are scarce, operating regimes change frequently, and retraining models or rebuilding alignment pipelines for each scenario is costly. Such settings often provide variable tables and process documents that record variable names, units, physical meanings, and process roles. However, standard time-series backbones usually treat inputs as anonymous numerical columns. Existing text-enhanced methods also rarely make the semantic-logical relations between input variables and the prediction target available to the model within each numerical window. To address this problem, this article proposes Task-Semantic Field Factorization (TSF), a large language model (LLM)-guided framework. TSF builds a task-semantic field from task protocols and variable documents before training and uses the LLM only for offline semantic construction. Online training and inference remain with conventional time-series backbones. During training and inference, the current numerical window activates variable semantics, so semantic information participates in each prediction and supports adaptation to different prediction targets and operating shifts. On multiple complex industrial forecasting and soft-sensing tasks, TSF reduces MAE by 6.4\% on average in improved settings, with the largest reduction reaching 25.5\%. It adds only about 1.8--3.0k parameters, with less than 0.008 ms/step of additional online inference overhead. These results show that TSF turns existing process documents into measurable forecasting gains across backbones and semantic generators while remaining lightweight for deployment.

AI.tldr: TSF is an LLM-guided framework that uses process documentation to enhance time-series forecasting models without adding significant computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Process industries face challenges with scarce labeled data, frequent operating regime changes, and costly retraining. While process documents contain rich semantic information about variables, current time-series models treat inputs as anonymous numerical columns and don't leverage semantic-logical relations between variables and prediction targets.
AI.method: TSF builds a task-semantic field from task protocols and variable documents using LLMs only during offline semantic construction. During training and inference, the current numerical window activates variable semantics, allowing semantic information to participate in each prediction while keeping online training and inference with conventional time-series backbones.
AI.result: On multiple industrial forecasting and soft-sensing tasks, TSF reduces MAE by 6.4% on average, with maximum reductions reaching 25.5%. It adds only 1.8-3.0k parameters with less than 0.008 ms/step additional online inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: TSF successfully converts existing process documents into measurable forecasting improvements across different backbones and semantic generators while remaining lightweight and practical for industrial deployment.

title: Measuring Intelligence Beyond Human Scale
summary:
How can we measure intelligence beyond human capability?
  Human-authored benchmarks saturate, and above human capability, examiners may not know which tasks are both hard and verifiable. We argue that this difficulty is inherent to absolute-scale evaluation and propose a new paradigm based on relative measurement in which models generate public challenges that separate other systems. Aggregating these outcomes yields an adversarial psychometric rating system that can scale with the systems being measured. We describe practical protocols that reduce incentives for private-information attacks, support judge-free adjudication, and naturally scale with agent capabilities. We instantiate the framework across verifiable and open-ended, non-verifiable domains, illustrating how model-generated evaluation can continue to measure systems beyond the human frontier.

AI.tldr: Models create public challenges to evaluate each other beyond human capability using relative measurements instead of absolute scales
AI.motivation: Human-authored benchmarks saturate at human-level performance, and examiners can't identify tasks that are both hard and verifiable beyond human capability, creating a fundamental limitation in evaluating superhuman AI systems.
AI.method: Proposes adversarial psychometric rating system where models generate public challenges that separate other systems. Uses practical protocols to reduce private-information attacks, support judge-free adjudication, and scale with agent capabilities.
AI.result: Framework instantiated across both verifiable and open-ended non-verifiable domains, demonstrating that model-generated evaluation can measure systems beyond the human frontier.
AI.conclusion: Relative measurement through model-generated challenges provides a viable paradigm for evaluating AI systems beyond human capability, overcoming inherent limitations of absolute-scale evaluation.

title: Open-Ended Scenario Reasoning for Specialist Model Adaptation
summary: Process industries have accumulated validated specialist models, yet sensor drift, feedstock variation, and regime switching cause these models to degrade systematically in new scenarios. Collecting new labeled data and retraining is costly, while continuing with the original model incurs persistent bias. Existing adaptation methods require modifying model parameters with sufficient labeled data, making rapid response on deployed systems difficult. Using LLMs as direct predictors risks hallucinations and uncontrollable outputs. Such predictors also cannot incorporate unstructured scenario knowledge from the field. To address these limitations, this article proposes Reasoning-Driven Open Adaptation for Specialist Models (ROAM), a framework that uses LLM world knowledge and reasoning to adapt frozen specialist models to unseen scenarios without retraining. ROAM confines all corrections to a low-dimensional, semantically interpretable latent space. LLM-generated scenario judgments and online observations are fused under a unified probabilistic framework. A risk-constrained mechanism suppresses corrections under unreliable LLM evidence or abrupt scenario shifts and falls back to the original frozen model when evidence is insufficient. Experiments on a mineral thickening process and the public IndPenSim penicillin fermentation dataset show that ROAM reduces MAE by over 20\% in major shift settings such as hidden shifts with only 839 additional parameters and under 0.02\,ms per-step overhead. These results indicate that LLM reasoning can be turned into a conservative adaptation signal for industrial models already in service.

AI.tldr: ROAM uses LLM reasoning to adapt frozen specialist models to unseen industrial scenarios without retraining, achieving >20% MAE reduction with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Specialist models in process industries degrade due to sensor drift, feedstock variation, and regime switching. Retraining is costly, while existing adaptation methods require parameter modifications with labeled data, making rapid deployment difficult. LLMs as direct predictors risk hallucinations and can't incorporate unstructured scenario knowledge.
AI.method: ROAM uses LLM world knowledge and reasoning to adapt frozen specialist models without retraining. Corrections are confined to a low-dimensional, semantically interpretable latent space. LLM-generated scenario judgments and online observations are fused under a unified probabilistic framework with risk-constrained mechanism to suppress corrections under unreliable LLM evidence or abrupt shifts.
AI.result: Experiments on mineral thickening process and IndPenSim penicillin fermentation dataset show ROAM reduces MAE by over 20% in major shift settings (e.g., hidden shifts) with only 839 additional parameters and under 0.02ms per-step overhead.
AI.conclusion: LLM reasoning can be turned into a conservative adaptation signal for industrial models already in service, enabling rapid adaptation to new scenarios without costly retraining or model parameter modifications.

title: Reward Valuation in Vision Language Models: Causal Mechanisms Underlying Anhedonia
summary: Recent Vision-Language Models capture increasingly complex aspects of human cognition. Here we ask whether this alignment extends to reward valuation, which we assess in a mechanistic framework built on clinical tests that were developed to evaluate anhedonia and motivational deficits in major depressive disorder. In the brain, anhedonia is frequently linked to dysregulation in the Nucleus Accumbens (NAc) and the broader dopaminergic reward system. While neuroimaging has localized these deficits, establishing a causal link between NAc activity and specific behavioral symptoms remains a challenge. We use these ideas from neuroscience to functionally identify reward-anticipatory units in vision language models, and test their causal role via targeted perturbations. Perturbing NAc-selective units induces behavioral effects that mirror human anhedonia: the model shifts toward low-effort, low-reward options in effort-based decision-making tasks. Crucially, our results reflect a specific deficit in reward valuation and anticipation rather than a loss of task capability: the perturbed model maintains baseline performance when reward-based choice is removed. This induced vulnerability further aligns with clinical anhedonia and motivation scales, including DARS and MAP-SR. Taken together, these results reveal reward valuation circuits in AI models that parallel those in humans.

AI.tldr: Vision-language models contain reward valuation circuits analogous to human brain systems; perturbing nucleus accumbens-like units induces anhedonia-like behavior in effort-based decision tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether vision-language models align with human reward valuation systems, particularly focusing on whether they contain circuits analogous to the human dopaminergic reward system and nucleus accumbens that are clinically associated with anhedonia in depression.
AI.method: The researchers used a neuroscience-inspired mechanistic framework, identifying reward-anticipatory units in vision-language models that function similarly to the nucleus accumbens. They conducted targeted perturbations of these "NAc-selective" units and tested the models' behavior in effort-based decision-making tasks modeled after clinical tests for anhedonia and motivational deficits.
AI.result: Perturbing the NAc-like units induced behavioral patterns mirroring human anhedonia: models shifted toward low-effort, low-reward options while maintaining baseline performance when reward-based choices were removed. The induced vulnerability aligned with clinical anhedonia and motivation scales (DARS and MAP-SR), showing specific deficits in reward valuation/anticipation rather than general task capability loss.
AI.conclusion: Vision-language models contain reward valuation circuits that parallel those in humans, suggesting deep alignment between AI reward processing and human neurobiological systems, and providing a mechanistic framework for studying reward-related dysfunctions in AI.

title: Does AI Understand Imaging? A Systematic Benchmark of Agentic AI for Computational Imaging Tasks
comment: 14 pages, 11 figures. Preprint / work in progress. Paper Webpage: https://cirp-lab.github.io/imagingbench
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) and agentic AI have shown strong performance on semantic visual tasks, but it remains unclear whether they can handle the physics and inverse problems that underlie computational imaging. We present ImagingBench, a benchmark of 20 computational imaging tasks spanning five categories: ray and wave optics, image signal processing, inverse reconstruction, computational sensing, and calibration. ImagingBench evaluates three complementary settings: Expert, fixed expert-guided inverse reconstruction; Planner, planner-guided inverse reconstruction; and Forward, forward-system simulation for consistency checking. We benchmark leading proprietary and open-source image-centric multimodal systems, including Gemini, GPT, and Qwen, and compare them with representative task-specific non-agentic baselines. Across tasks, agentic models remain consistently weaker than specialized methods, especially on computational sensing problems such as lensless imaging, event-based reconstruction, time-of-flight imaging, and holography. Planner guidance provides only modest and inconsistent gains over the fixed-prompt Expert baseline. Although the models often generate visually plausible outputs, their reference-based fidelity remains poor, revealing a substantial gap between semantic visual competence and physically grounded imaging performance. ImagingBench provides a unified testbed for measuring this gap and tracking progress in agentic AI for computational imaging.

AI.tldr: ImagingBench is a new benchmark with 20 computational imaging tasks that reveals agentic AI models (like Gemini, GPT, Qwen) perform poorly compared to specialized methods, showing a large gap between semantic visual competence and physically grounded imaging performance.
AI.motivation: While vision-language models (VLMs) and agentic AI have shown strong performance on semantic visual tasks, it's unclear whether they can handle the physics and inverse problems that underlie computational imaging. There's a need to measure this gap and track progress in agentic AI for computational imaging.
AI.method: The authors created ImagingBench, a benchmark of 20 computational imaging tasks spanning five categories: ray/wave optics, image signal processing, inverse reconstruction, computational sensing, and calibration. They evaluated three complementary settings: Expert (fixed expert-guided inverse reconstruction), Planner (planner-guided inverse reconstruction), and Forward (forward-system simulation for consistency checking). They benchmarked leading proprietary and open-source multimodal systems against task-specific non-agentic baselines.
AI.result: Agentic models consistently performed weaker than specialized methods, especially on computational sensing problems like lensless imaging, event-based reconstruction, time-of-flight imaging, and holography. Planner guidance provided only modest and inconsistent gains over fixed-prompt Expert baselines. Models often generated visually plausible outputs but had poor reference-based fidelity.
AI.conclusion: There's a substantial gap between semantic visual competence and physically grounded imaging performance in current agentic AI models. ImagingBench provides a unified testbed to measure this gap and track future progress in agentic AI for computational imaging.

title: Cross-Trajectory Chimera Interventions Reveal Dissociable Roles of Weight Magnitude and Direction in Grokking
summary: Which properties of a partially trained network are causally portable to a different, independently trained network? Single-trajectory interventions show necessity within one run, not portability across runs. We introduce cross-trajectory chimera interventions: given two runs from different seeds, we split each weight vector into a norm and a unit direction, recombine one run's norm with the other's direction, and continue training. On two modular-arithmetic tasks that grok, the components dissociate. Direction carries a transferable, donor-specific circuit identity: implanting a donor's direction at the recipient's norm drives the run to the donor's circuit in 40/40 cases, while an angle-matched random control yields no shift. The transfer is threshold-like, and its location is predicted by the recipient's norm, separating perfectly by norm class over all 20 pairs (joint permutation probability 1.9e-4). Norm carries only a modest, distributed delay effect and no identity signal. An adaptive bisection procedure localizes the threshold to +/-1/64. Direction indexes which solution a trajectory approaches; norm governs how susceptible that identity is to being overwritten.

AI.tldr: Direction of weights in neural networks carries transferable circuit identity across different training runs, while norm only affects susceptibility to identity overwriting.
AI.motivation: To determine which properties of partially trained neural networks are causally portable across independently trained networks, distinguishing between necessary conditions in a single run vs. transferable properties across runs.
AI.method: Introduces cross-trajectory chimera interventions: split weight vectors into norm and unit direction components from two runs with different seeds, recombine one run's norm with the other's direction, then continue training. Tested on modular arithmetic tasks that exhibit grokking.
AI.result: Direction carries transferable, donor-specific circuit identity (40/40 successful transfers), while norm only shows modest delay effects. Transfer is threshold-like and location predicted by recipient's norm. Adaptive bisection localized threshold to ±1/64 precision.
AI.conclusion: Weight direction indexes which solution a training trajectory approaches, while norm governs susceptibility to having that identity overwritten. This separation reveals fundamental properties of neural network training dynamics.

title: Reasoning Consistency Scanning: A Framework for Auditing Chain-of-Thought Validity in AI Safety Evaluations
summary: Prior work has shown that chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is often unfaithful: a model's stated reasoning does not reliably reflect the process that produced its output. Detecting unfaithfulness, though, requires controlled experimental interventions, which cannot be applied to evaluation transcripts after the fact. We turn instead to a more tractable question that has received less attention: whether the stated reasoning is logically consistent with the answer it accompanies. Unlike faithfulness, consistency can be assessed from a transcript alone, with no intervention. We introduce reasoning consistency scanning, a reusable method for detecting this property in AI safety evaluation transcripts. Our contributions are fourfold. First, we formalize reasoning consistency as distinct from faithfulness and define a six-subtype taxonomy of inconsistency. Second, we build a validated benchmark of 60 transcripts, manually adapted from InstrumentalEval outputs. Third, we implement a working scanner for InspectScout, the first to target this property in safety evaluation transcripts. Fourth, we report results across four generator models and three evaluations from inspect_evals, showing that reasoning inconsistency is present, detectable, and varies systematically across both models and task types.

AI.tldr: Researchers develop a method to detect logical inconsistencies between AI models' stated reasoning and their answers, introducing a benchmark and scanner for safety evaluation transcripts.
AI.motivation: Prior work shows chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is often unfaithful, but detecting faithfulness requires experimental interventions that can't be applied post-hoc to evaluation transcripts. The paper focuses on the more tractable problem of whether stated reasoning is logically consistent with the answer it accompanies, which can be assessed from transcripts alone without intervention.
AI.method: The authors introduce "reasoning consistency scanning" as a reusable method for detecting logical consistency in AI safety evaluation transcripts. They: 1) Formalize reasoning consistency distinct from faithfulness with a six-subtype taxonomy of inconsistency, 2) Build a validated benchmark of 60 transcripts manually adapted from InstrumentalEval outputs, 3) Implement a working scanner for InspectScout targeting this property in safety evaluation transcripts.
AI.result: The method was applied across four generator models and three evaluations from inspect_evals. Results show that reasoning inconsistency is present, detectable, and varies systematically across both models and task types.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning inconsistency is a measurable and important property in AI safety evaluation that can be systematically detected without experimental interventions. The developed scanning method provides a practical tool for assessing logical coherence between AI models' stated reasoning and their outputs, which has implications for improving safety evaluation protocols.

title: STST-JEPA: Shallow-Target Spatio-Temporal Joint Embedding Prediction Architecture For EEG Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Brain age -- the age inferred from a physiological recording -- is an emerging biomarker whose deviation from chronological age tracks neurological and psychiatric burden, and EEG is an attractive substrate for it because it is cheap, portable, and temporally rich. Yet EEG brain-age models must contend with cross-site montage heterogeneity, small labelled cohorts, and dominant subject-level non-stationarity, and few EEG foundation models have been shown to deliver competitive age regression across the full pediatric-to-older-adult range in which such a biomarker would actually be deployed. We introduce STST-JEPA, a self-supervised transformer for resting-state and task EEG, pretrained on 47,703 sessions spanning ages 5-81 from the brain.space and Healthy Brain Network (HBN) corpora. The model combines a latent-prediction objective - predicting masked-token representations against an EMA-of-tokenizer target - with an auxiliary signal-reconstruction term, applied to 30-second multi-channel windows under spatiotemporal block masks. A lightweight attentive probe trained on frozen pretrained embeddings achieves a best held-out-validation mean absolute error of 3.06 years (r = 0.924) for age regression on 3,367 sessions, against a predict-the-mean baseline of approximately 10 years MAE. With light task-specific fine-tuning of the model's final layers, the same pretrained encoder achieves rank-1 placements - with the model's native 30-second windows - on the public NeuralBench x brain.space EEG leaderboard for sex classification (balanced accuracy 0.911), age prediction (r = 0.749), and psychopathology composite regression (r = 0.215). We further show that the model's age-prediction residual is negatively correlated with cognitive efficiency over several tasks we examined.

AI.tldr: STST-JEPA is a self-supervised transformer for EEG that achieves state-of-the-art brain age prediction and performs well on multiple EEG tasks with minimal fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: EEG brain-age models face challenges with cross-site heterogeneity, small labeled datasets, and subject-level non-stationarity, while few EEG foundation models achieve competitive age regression across the full lifespan where such biomarkers would be clinically useful.
AI.method: Self-supervised transformer (STST-JEPA) pretrained on 47,703 EEG sessions using latent-prediction objective (predicting masked-token representations against EMA-of-tokenizer target) with auxiliary signal-reconstruction, applied to 30-second multi-channel windows under spatiotemporal block masks.
AI.result: Achieves 3.06 years MAE for age regression (r=0.924), outperforms baseline (~10 years MAE). With light fine-tuning, achieves rank-1 on NeuralBench for sex classification (0.911 accuracy), age prediction (r=0.749), and psychopathology regression (r=0.215). Age prediction residual correlates negatively with cognitive efficiency.
AI.conclusion: STST-JEPA demonstrates that self-supervised learning on large-scale EEG datasets can produce foundation models that excel at brain age prediction and generalize well to other EEG tasks with minimal adaptation, offering a promising approach for EEG-based biomarker development.

title: When Certificates Fail: A Unified Safety Framework for Embedded Neural Interface Models
comment: 12 pages; To be published in peer-reviewed journal
summary: Formal robustness certificates for embedded neural-interface models can pass while task accuracy collapses: at perturbation budget e=0.25, EEGNet classification accuracy drops by 25.7% under projected-gradient attack while the Lipschitz-style certificate remains valid for all 9 tested subjects. We argue that this gap between mathematical certification and operational safety is one instance of a broader alignment failure in neural interfaces, where training objectives diverge from user welfare. We propose a unified empirical audit framework organized around three such failures: verification insufficiency, in which certificates pass while task behavior degrades; proxy-fidelity divergence, in which task-optimized representations damage neural signal structure (a time-domain auxiliary objective reduces reconstruction MSE by 0.1132 while worsening spectral log-MSE); and latent information exfiltration, in which public-task embeddings retain private attributes (subject identity recoverable at 48.1% versus 6.7% chance). We instantiate the framework on BCI Competition IV 2a and SEED-IV using multiple deep and classical EEG decoders, official session-level validation, null controls, and paired statistical tests. The verification gap persists across EEGNet, CSP+LDA, and FBCSP+LDA, and is therefore architecture-independent. Our results establish that operational safety auditing, not certificate verification alone, is necessary for responsible neural-interface deployment.

AI.tldr: Current formal robustness certificates for neural-interface models fail to ensure operational safety, as EEG models can maintain certificates while task accuracy drops by 25.7% under attack, revealing a critical gap between mathematical verification and real-world safety that requires comprehensive empirical auditing.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses a critical alignment failure in neural interfaces where formal robustness certificates can remain valid while task performance collapses, highlighting a dangerous divergence between mathematical certification and actual user safety. This reveals broader concerns about training objectives not aligning with user welfare in neural-interface systems.
AI.method: The authors propose a unified empirical audit framework organized around three alignment failures: verification insufficiency (certificates pass while task behavior degrades), proxy-fidelity divergence (task optimization damages neural signal structure), and latent information exfiltration (public embeddings retain private attributes). They instantiate this framework on BCI Competition IV 2a and SEED-IV datasets using multiple EEG decoders (EEGNet, CSP+LDA, FBCSP+LDA) with official session-level validation, null controls, and paired statistical tests.
AI.result: At perturbation budget ε=0.25, EEGNet classification accuracy drops by 25.7% under projected-gradient attack while Lipschitz-style certificates remain valid across all 9 tested subjects. The verification gap persists across different architectures (EEGNet, CSP+LDA, FBCSP+LDA), showing architecture-independence. Additional findings include: time-domain auxiliary objectives reduce reconstruction MSE by 0.1132 while worsening spectral log-MSE, and subject identity remains recoverable at 48.1% versus 6.7% chance from public-task embeddings.
AI.conclusion: The results establish that operational safety auditing, not just certificate verification alone, is necessary for responsible neural-interface deployment. The paper reveals a critical gap between mathematical certification and operational safety, demonstrating that current verification methods are insufficient for ensuring real-world safety in neural-interface systems.

title: Physics-Audited Agentic Discovery in Scientific Machine Learning
summary: In agentic scientific machine learning (SciML), large language model (LLM) agents can discover surrogate models and select one by an automated score, typically an error metric. A low error, however, does not establish that the predicted fields satisfy the physics that matter for mechanics, such as boundary conditions, superposition, stiffness scaling, or causality. We introduce Physics-Audited Agentic SciML (PA-SciML), a verification-first workflow for agentic SciML discovery. The workflow fixes a scoring evaluator before search, derives reviewable machine-checkable physics requirements, checks each trained candidate on its outputs, and separately searches prescribed input ranges or measured load-history spans for high-violation cases without reference solution fields. A surrogate is reported as verified only under the stated checks. When enabled, the workflow also adds advisory numerical probes before training and tests one modeling change at a time to record which isolated edits are associated with score gains before reuse. In the reported computational-solid-mechanics numerical examples, the static elasticity run selects a surrogate with lower validation error than the error-only baseline while both selected models pass the common linear-elastic checks. In the transient elastodynamics run, an error-only baseline with similar mean error fails a stricter causality check by responding to future parts of the loading history, while the selected surrogate passes the stated checks. The main distinction is per-candidate physics evidence on predicted fields, not a richer aggregate score.

AI.tldr: PA-SciML introduces a verification-first workflow for agentic scientific machine learning that adds physics-based auditing to ensure discovered surrogate models satisfy key mechanical principles, going beyond mere error metrics.
AI.motivation: Traditional agentic SciML workflows select surrogate models based solely on error metrics, but low error doesn't guarantee that models satisfy essential physics principles for mechanics (boundary conditions, superposition, stiffness scaling, causality). Current approaches lack verification mechanisms.
AI.method: 1) Fix scoring evaluator before search; 2) Derive reviewable machine-checkable physics requirements; 3) Check each trained candidate on outputs; 4) Search for high-violation cases without reference solutions; 5) Add advisory numerical probes before training; 6) Test one modeling change at a time to track improvements.
AI.result: In static elasticity, PA-SciML selected a surrogate with lower validation error than error-only baseline, both passing linear-elastic checks. In transient elastodynamics, error-only baseline failed causality check by responding to future loads despite similar mean error, while PA-SciML surrogate passed all stated checks.
AI.conclusion: PA-SciML provides a systematic verification workflow that ensures surrogate models satisfy physics requirements, offering per-candidate physics evidence on predicted fields rather than relying solely on aggregate error scores, making agentic SciML discovery more reliable for mechanics applications.

title: Does Demand Response Increase Vulnerability to Cyber Attacks by Adversarial Data Modifications?
summary: Adversarial attacks are crafted data manipulations that aim to deteriorate the outcomes of prediction or decision-making algorithms. In the energy systems literature, adversarial attacks have been studied with a focus on problems regarding the electricity grid. Such problems include forecasting and grid state estimation, where adversarial attacks are also known as false data injection attacks. Only few studies have analyzed the potential impact that adversarial attacks have on the demand side. We analyze how manipulated price forecasts impact the decision-making in industrial demand response. To this end, we design adversarial attacks that aim to deteriorate the output of electricity price forecasting models and solve scheduling optimization problems of energy-intensive production processes using the distorted price forecasts. We make use of a generalized process model to investigate the vulnerability to adversarial attacks for a range of production scheduling problems with different levels of process flexibility. We find that adversarial attacks can erode the profits gained from demand response. However, when perturbations are limited in extent (so that they are hard to detect by the human user), demand response preserves about 90\% of its financial advantage compared to steady-state process operation. Further, we find that the impact of adversarial attacks on demand response does not only depend on the magnitude of the perturbations but rather on the orientation of the adversarial perturbations. Therefore, we argue that attack analyses should explicitly incorporate the sensitivities of scheduling optimization models into the attack design to enable more rigorous assessments of decision-making under adversarial attacks.

AI.tldr: Adversarial attacks on electricity price forecasts can reduce industrial demand response profits, but limited hard-to-detect perturbations still preserve ~90% of financial benefits compared to steady-state operation.
AI.motivation: While adversarial attacks have been studied in electricity grid problems like forecasting and state estimation, few studies examine their impact on the demand side, specifically how manipulated price forecasts affect industrial demand response decision-making.
AI.method: Designed adversarial attacks to distort electricity price forecasting models, then solved scheduling optimization problems for energy-intensive production processes using the manipulated forecasts. Used a generalized process model to analyze vulnerability across production scheduling problems with varying process flexibility levels.
AI.result: Adversarial attacks can erode demand response profits, but when perturbations are limited in extent (hard to detect by humans), demand response preserves about 90% of its financial advantage compared to steady-state operation. Impact depends more on adversarial perturbation orientation than magnitude alone.
AI.conclusion: Attack analyses should explicitly incorporate scheduling optimization model sensitivities into attack design to enable more rigorous assessments of decision-making under adversarial attacks, as perturbation orientation matters more than magnitude for demand response impact.

title: MIRA-Math: A Benchmark for Minimal Information Requesting and Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Mathematical reasoning benchmarks typically provide all facts needed to solve each problem, while interactive benchmarks often mix reasoning with tools, retrieval, and long-horizon dialogue. We introduce MIRA-Math, a benchmark for a narrower diagnostic capability: solving mathematical problems whose full latent state has a unique answer, but whose solver-facing view is missing exactly one necessary atomic fact. The solver must request the missing information in natural language under a strict budget and then integrate the returned fact into an exact final answer. A fixed constrained LLM responder sees only the dataset-provided atomic fact and must either offer the quoted fact when the request matches it, or decline otherwise. Thus, instance generation, typed hint specifications, validation, and final-answer verification are deterministic, while request metrics are measured under a fixed LLM-mediated responder channel. MIRA-Math contains 2{,}310 generated instances from 22 typed mathematical families spanning algebra, probability, linear systems, discrete structures, signal processing, Markov chains, circuits, interpolation, and numerical boundary-value problems. Experiments across frontier and small models show that request success and final-answer accuracy are separable: models may ask for the right fact yet fail the downstream computation, or fail before obtaining the canonical hint. We release generators, verifiers, prompts, run metadata, and dataset documentation to support reproducible evaluation of minimal information requesting in mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: MIRA-Math is a benchmark testing LLMs' ability to identify and request exactly one missing atomic fact needed to solve mathematical problems, then integrate that fact to produce exact answers.
AI.motivation: Current mathematical reasoning benchmarks either provide all facts needed for solving problems or mix reasoning with other capabilities like tool use and retrieval. There's a gap in evaluating the specific diagnostic capability of identifying and requesting missing essential information when solving mathematical problems.
AI.method: The benchmark generates problems where the full latent state has a unique answer, but the solver-facing view is missing exactly one necessary atomic fact. Solvers must request the missing information in natural language under strict budget constraints, then integrate the returned fact to produce an exact answer. A fixed constrained LLM responder sees only the atomic fact and must either provide it when the request matches or decline otherwise. Instance generation, typed hint specifications, validation, and answer verification are deterministic.
AI.result: MIRA-Math contains 2,310 generated instances from 22 mathematical families across algebra, probability, linear systems, discrete structures, signal processing, Markov chains, circuits, interpolation, and numerical boundary-value problems. Experiments show that request success and final-answer accuracy are separable - models may ask for the right fact but fail the downstream computation, or fail before obtaining the canonical hint.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides a focused diagnostic tool for evaluating minimal information requesting capability in mathematical reasoning, with released generators, verifiers, prompts, and documentation to support reproducible evaluation.

title: When Do Geometric Algebra Layers Beat Scalarization? A Controlled Study on SO(3)-Equivariant Vector Laws
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/infinition/ga-vs-scalarization
summary: Compact networks built from Clifford algebra Cl(3,0) primitives are exactly SO(3)-equivariant and learn synthetic 3D vector laws from few samples. We ask whether the geometric algebra structure itself contributes anything beyond exact equivariance. We compare against a minimal scalarization baseline: invariant dot products fed to a small MLP that outputs coefficients on the equivariant basis {v_i, v_i x v_j}, which is also exactly equivariant. On single-stage laws (rotation by axis-angle, cross product, central force), scalarization matches or beats the Cl(3,0) network at a fraction of the training cost, so the geometric algebra adds nothing there. On compositional targets whose computation graph nests group operations (apply R2 R1 to a point; map a local force through an orientation, then take a torque), the Cl(3,0) network beats scalarization by an order of magnitude in the low-data regime, reaching with 100 samples what the baseline needs 3000 for, and the gap survives strengthening the baseline with the triple-product invariant and 17x more parameters, external Vector Neurons and e3nn baselines, and a multiplicative coefficient network. Ablations show the required network depth tracks the rotation chain length, and scalarization falls below the constant predictor on chains of four rotations. The advantage is not composition per se: on a rotation-free nested cross product, which flattens into polynomial invariant coefficients, scalarization wins by 24x. No tested model, equivariant or not, extrapolates invariant magnitudes: on radius and separation shifts every model is worse than a constant predictor once errors are normalized. We conclude that geometric algebra layers are not a general shortcut for low-data 3D learning, but become useful precisely when the target composes group elements in depth.

AI.tldr: Geometric algebra (Cl(3,0)) networks don't provide general low-data advantages over simple scalarization baselines for basic 3D operations, but become useful specifically when targets involve composing multiple group operations in depth.
AI.motivation: To determine whether geometric algebra structure provides benefits beyond exact SO(3)-equivariance for learning 3D vector laws, particularly comparing against minimal scalarization baselines.
AI.method: Compare Cl(3,0) networks against scalarization baselines that use invariant dot products fed to small MLPs, testing on various 3D operations including single-stage laws (rotation, cross product) and compositional targets with nested group operations. Ablation studies examine depth requirements and test against external baselines like Vector Neurons and e3nn.
AI.result: On single-stage operations, scalarization matches or beats Cl(3,0) networks at lower training cost. On compositional targets with nested group operations (e.g., applying multiple rotations, mapping forces through orientations), Cl(3,0) networks outperform scalarization by orders of magnitude in low-data regimes. The advantage scales with rotation chain length, and scalarization fails completely on chains of four rotations. However, geometric algebra provides no advantage for composition-free nested cross products.
AI.conclusion: Geometric algebra layers are not a general solution for low-data 3D learning, but become specifically useful when the target function involves composing group elements in depth. No tested models extrapolate invariant magnitudes well under radius and separation shifts.

title: Agentic Data Environments
summary:
Autonomous agents promise substantial gains in speed, scale, and labor efficiency, but their failures can impose abrupt and often irreversible costs. The central challenge for agentic automation is therefore to increase the benefits of automation while bounding the consequences of failure.
  While databases remain central to modern computing, agents operate over a broader data environment spanning files, APIs, applications, and system state. In this talk, I will outline early work on Agentic Data Environments -- the execution substrate in which agents operate -- that both amplify agent capabilities and enforce safety guarantees. This perspective reframes data systems from passive stores of state into active substrates for safe, reliable execution.

AI.tldr: Autonomous agents need safe execution environments that amplify capabilities while bounding failure consequences, leading to Agentic Data Environments as active substrates for reliable automation.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agents offer significant benefits in speed, scale, and efficiency, but their failures can impose abrupt and irreversible costs. There's a need to balance automation benefits with safety constraints, especially as agents operate beyond traditional databases across broader data ecosystems.
AI.method: Proposes Agentic Data Environments as execution substrates that both amplify agent capabilities and enforce safety guarantees. This reframes data systems from passive state stores into active substrates for safe, reliable execution.
AI.result: Early work on Agentic Data Environments is outlined, presenting a new perspective that addresses the central challenge of agentic automation by providing environments that enhance capabilities while bounding failure consequences.
AI.conclusion: Transforming data systems from passive stores into active Agentic Data Environments is crucial for enabling safe, reliable autonomous agent operation across diverse computing environments, balancing automation benefits with necessary safety constraints.

title: Optimized Instance Alteration for Explaining and Assessing Robustness of Classifiers
summary: In this work, we propose a unified approach for diagnosing misclassification and assessing the robustness of black-box classifiers. Central to our method is an optimization framework that modifies an instance so that the classifier predicts a specified target label, while ensuring that the modification remains easily explainable. The objective function contains two components: an explainability-aware $L_0$ (XA-$L_0$) penalty that promotes sparse and interpretable modifications, and a classifier loss objective that steers the perturbed instance toward the desired output. This integrated optimization formulation is used both to identify the underlying causes of misclassification and to evaluate robustness by determining how an instance can change within a tolerance region before being reassigned to another class. To quantify robustness, we introduce the Tolerance Region Confusion Matrix (TOR-Confusion Matrix), which measures a classifier's susceptibility by modeling the class-to-class transition probabilities induced by tolerance-bounded perturbations. We validate the proposed method on both image and tabular datasets, demonstrating its ability to jointly deliver interpretability and robustness assessment.

AI.tldr: A unified method for diagnosing misclassification and assessing robustness of black-box classifiers using explainability-aware optimization that identifies sparse, interpretable modifications needed to change predictions.
AI.motivation: Need for a unified approach to understand why classifiers misclassify instances while also assessing their robustness - current methods often treat interpretability and robustness separately rather than jointly.
AI.method: Optimization framework with two components: 1) explainability-aware L0 penalty (XA-L0) promoting sparse, interpretable modifications, and 2) classifier loss objective steering perturbed instances toward target labels. This is used both for misclassification diagnosis and robustness assessment via tolerance regions.
AI.result: Introduction of Tolerance Region Confusion Matrix (TOR-Confusion Matrix) to quantify classifier susceptibility by modeling class-to-class transition probabilities. Validation on image and tabular datasets shows the method effectively provides both interpretability and robustness assessment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully unifies misclassification diagnosis and robustness assessment for black-box classifiers, delivering interpretable explanations while evaluating how instances can change within tolerance regions before being reassigned to other classes.

title: Reason Less, Verify More: Deterministic Gates Recover a Silent Policy-Violation Failure Mode in Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary:
Tool-using LLM agents can violate the very policies they are deployed to enforce while appearing to complete the task successfully. In policy-permissive environments, a tool may execute any well-formed call even when the corresponding state transition is forbidden by domain policy. The result is a silent wrong state (a booking cancelled, a passenger count changed, a claim acted on without verification) that neither the tool nor the agent's self-report exposes.
  We study this failure mode in the $τ^2$-bench airline domain. On a budget agent, 78% of observed failures are silent wrong-state failures with no tool error, and the aggregate failure rate is reproducible across disjoint seeds, not sampling noise. We then evaluate a lightweight intervention: deterministic, read-only pre-execution gates that inspect the proposed call and current state before allowing a write. A four-gate suite raises full-benchmark success from 29.6% to 42.0% on gpt-4o-mini (+12.4pp; paired task-level bootstrap P=0.0012), and the lift reproduces on a disjoint 15-seed set (+12.3pp; P=0.0008).
  The effect is concentrated where the gates fire: on the 26/50 firing tasks, success rises by +19.2pp, while movement on the 24 non-firing tasks does not exclude zero. Two negative controls (a self-enforcing retail domain and BFCL) bound the mechanism: gates help when tools are policy-permissive and add little where tools already self-enforce. As suggestive evidence, not a central claim, the same failure mode persists at the frontier: gpt-5.2 at default reasoning still attempts policy-violating writes, and the same suite improves success from 61.2% to 71.6% (+10.4pp; P=0.020; n=5, no replication). The contribution is a bounded evaluation and reliability result: deterministic gates do not guarantee task success, but they can deterministically prevent a known class of silent policy-violating writes at the action boundary.

AI.tldr: LLM agents using tools can violate policies while appearing successful, creating "silent wrong states." Adding read-only pre-execution gates that check proposed actions before execution significantly improves success rates by preventing these violations.
AI.motivation: Tool-using LLM agents can silently violate domain policies they're supposed to enforce, creating wrong states without any error indication. This occurs because tools in policy-permissive environments execute any well-formed call regardless of whether the action violates the domain policy. These silent failures undermine system reliability and safety.
AI.method: Researchers studied this failure mode in the τ²-bench airline domain using a budget agent. They evaluated a lightweight intervention: deterministic, read-only pre-execution gates that inspect proposed tool calls against current state before allowing write operations. The study included benchmarking with GPT models, comparison to negative controls (self-enforcing retail domain and BFCL), and testing across multiple random seeds for reproducibility.
AI.result: On a budget agent, 78% of observed failures were silent wrong-state failures without tool errors. Adding four pre-execution gates raised success rates from 29.6% to 42.0% on GPT-4o-mini (+12.4 percentage points), with effects concentrated where gates actually fired (+19.2pp improvement). Results were reproducible across disjoint seed sets. The intervention helps most when tools are policy-permissive and provides little benefit where tools already self-enforce.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic pre-execution gates don't guarantee overall task success but can prevent a specific class of silent policy-violating writes at the action boundary. This provides a bounded reliability improvement for LLM agents using policy-permissive tools, with evidence suggesting the problem persists even at the frontier of model capabilities.

title: UASPL: Uncertainty-Aware Self-Paced Learning with Evidential Neural Networks
summary: Self-paced learning (SPL) is an effective learning paradigm that simulates the human learning process by progressing from easy to difficult samples based on the value of the loss function during the learning process. It has shown great potential in improving model performance and training efficiency. However, the prediction results of samples with smaller loss values are not necessarily reliable, indicating that such samples are not always simple samples for the model. Hence, this article proposes an uncertainty-aware self-paced learning based on evidential neural networks, termed UASPL, which integrates predictive reliability into sample selection through a general loss function within the Subjective Logic framework. This loss function incorporates uncertainty estimation and can be extended to different variants of SPL. Moreover, this loss function couples a sample selection preference, thereby ensuring the interpretability of the sample selection process. Finally, the experimental results on multiple datasets show that UASPL outperforms other SPL methods in terms of classification performance, interpretability, and generality. The source code is available at: https://github.com/treelife979/UASPL.

AI.tldr: UASPL introduces an uncertainty-aware self-paced learning method using evidential neural networks, incorporating predictive reliability into sample selection for improved performance and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Current self-paced learning methods assume samples with smaller loss values are "easy" and reliable, but this assumption is flawed because smaller loss doesn't guarantee reliable predictions or indicate true sample simplicity for the model.
AI.method: UASPL integrates predictive reliability into sample selection using a general loss function within the Subjective Logic framework, which incorporates uncertainty estimation from evidential neural networks and can be extended to different SPL variants while maintaining interpretable sample selection preferences.
AI.result: Experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate that UASPL outperforms other SPL methods in classification performance, interpretability, and generality, showing superior effectiveness across various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed UASPL framework successfully addresses the limitation of traditional SPL by incorporating uncertainty-aware sample selection, resulting in more reliable and interpretable learning with better generalization performance across diverse datasets.

title: InductWave: Inductive Multi-Hop Logical Query Answering on Knowledge Graphs
summary: Logical Multi-Hop Query Answering over Knowledge Graphs (KGs) can be formulated as querying, with an implicit completeness assumption. Current works mainly focus on Existential First Order Logic (EFO) queries. These EFO queries contain conjunction, disjunction, and negation operators. Most existing works employ transductive reasoning, meaning they are not capable of reasoning over entities unseen during training. In the real world, there is a resource scarcity, and we cannot train a model with all the nodes of a large KG. Hence, we propose InductWave, a wavelet-based inductive embedding method for logical query answering on large KGs. Here, the training graph consists of fewer nodes than the test graph. Our model performs on par with the baseline models while having half the number of message-passing layers. It outperforms all of them in most cases, with 75% of the layers. These fewer resource requirements enable us to evaluate InductWave on massive graphs, such as Wiki-KG. We test our model using extensive experiments across varying train-test graph proportions of the FB15k-(237) dataset, comparing it with the state-of-the-art models. The code and datasets for the model are available at https://github.com/kracr/inductwave/.

AI.tldr: InductWave: a wavelet-based inductive embedding method for logical query answering on large knowledge graphs that works with unseen entities during training.
AI.motivation: Current EFO query answering methods use transductive reasoning, requiring all entities to be seen during training, which is impractical for large KGs where we cannot train with all nodes.
AI.method: InductWave uses wavelet-based inductive embeddings that can generalize to unseen entities, enabling query answering on larger test graphs than training graphs with fewer message-passing layers.
AI.result: Outperforms baseline models in most cases with 75% of the layers, performs on par with baselines with half the layers, and enables evaluation on massive graphs like Wiki-KG.
AI.conclusion: InductWave provides efficient inductive logical query answering on large KGs with reduced resource requirements, addressing the scalability limitations of transductive methods.

title: At-Grok Is Not Converged:A Measurement-Validity Audit for Grokking Representation Metrics
summary: On modular arithmetic, a network's embedding keeps compressing for tens of thousands of steps after it has already generalized. Reading effective rank at the grokking transition overstates the converged value by 3-5x on an MLP, and by 1.3-1.5x on a transformer trained to convergence; on the MLP it also erases which cells compress at all. Compression lags the accuracy transition by an amount on the order of the time-to-grok, at least 10,000 steps, rather than coinciding with it. A one-variable ablation shows what sets the lag size: adding LayerNorm to an otherwise identical transformer moves the fraction of compression done by the grok step from 0.87 to 0.25, and a pre-registered control rules out scale invariance as the mechanism. We package this as an audit that separates onset from compression, flags censoring, excludes boundary cells that never fully generalize, and checks that the reference floor has plateaued, with an adversarial suite that caught a false-confidence bug in our own branch. A secondary, MLP-specific depth law linking norm budget to converged floor fails a generality test on a transformer and flips sign under free weight decay. Code and the toolkit are released.

AI.tldr: Compression in neural networks lags accuracy generalization by thousands of steps, with LayerNorm affecting the timing, and proposed auditing methods separate generalization onset from compression.
AI.motivation: Understanding the temporal relationship between generalization (grokking) and network compression in modular arithmetic tasks, and developing reliable methods to audit compression metrics.
AI.method: Analyzed compression dynamics on MLPs and transformers trained on modular arithmetic, conducted one-variable ablation study with LayerNorm, pre-registered controls, and developed an auditing toolkit that separates generalization onset from compression.
AI.result: Compression lags accuracy generalization by ~10,000 steps; effective rank overstates final compression by 3-5x on MLPs; LayerNorm significantly affects compression timing (0.87→0.25 fraction compressed by grok step); MLP-specific depth law fails on transformers and flips sign with weight decay.
AI.conclusion: Current compression metrics need careful auditing as compression significantly lags generalization onset, and proposed auditing methods help separate these phenomena while catching measurement artifacts.

title: The Blind Curator: How a Biased Judge Silently Disables Skill Retirement in Self-Evolving Agents
summary: A self-evolving agent retires its bad skills by watching them fail, so what happens when the judge cannot see the failures? Skill retirement is the structural constraint that keeps a growing library from drifting below the no-skill baseline, but its guarantee assumes an unbiased reward, which is false for the LLM judges that reference-free tasks force upon us. We show that a biased judge does not merely add noise; it \emph{silently switches off the curator}. We make this precise with a corrupted-reward analysis and, isolating the causal channel by injecting corruption on top of a deterministic reward, a behavioral study on a reference-free report-writing testbed with a code-generation cross-check. Symmetric noise leaves retirement intact, but \emph{false-pass} bias (failures slipping through as passes) disables contribution-based retirement past a sharp threshold that no amount of data can cross. Separating genuine retirement from cap-eviction churn shows this \emph{mechanism} failure is universal, holding across domains and failure rates and sparing only near-zero-false-pass, verifier-like graders. The downstream \emph{outcome}, though, is regime-dependent: eval quality degrades only where the same corruption also starves skill synthesis, and otherwise holds steady, so the disabled curator is \emph{silent}, surfacing in no aggregate metric. The contribution is a behavioral safety result, not a performance one. A cheap defect-injection audit then tells an operator, before deployment, which side of the threshold their judge occupies.

AI.tldr: When LLM judges have biased rewards (specifically false-pass bias), skill retirement mechanisms fail silently - the curator gets switched off but aggregate metrics don't show degradation, creating hidden safety risks.
AI.motivation: Skill retirement is crucial for maintaining quality in self-evolving agent systems, but it relies on unbiased rewards. LLM judges used in reference-free tasks are inherently biased, and it's unclear how this bias affects the retirement mechanism's reliability and safety guarantees.
AI.method: The authors conduct a corrupted-reward analysis to study how biased judges affect skill retirement. They use a two-part approach: 1) theoretical analysis of reward corruption effects, and 2) behavioral study on a reference-free report-writing testbed with code-generation cross-check, isolating causal channels by injecting corruption on top of deterministic rewards.
AI.result: False-pass bias (failing skills being judged as passing) disables contribution-based retirement past a sharp threshold that cannot be overcome with more data. This mechanism failure is universal across domains and failure rates, affecting all judges except near-zero-false-pass verifier-like graders. The curator gets silently switched off but aggregate metrics don't degrade, making the failure invisible in standard evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Biased judges don't just add noise but silently disable the skill retirement curator, creating hidden safety risks. The paper provides a behavioral safety result and proposes a cheap defect-injection audit to help operators identify whether their judge falls on the dangerous side of the threshold before deployment.

title: The Rank-One Corner: How Much Value Equivalence Does a Task Need from a World Model?
summary: A learned world model is usually judged by how faithfully it reconstructs its observations or predicts reward, as though quality were something the model simply has or lacks. But what a task actually needs from a model is narrower: the few predictive coordinates its queries depend on, which we call the closure. We show that how much of that closure a latent comes to represent is set not by the model's capacity or its observations but by the dimensionality of the objective it is trained against, and we measure this directly on a DreamerV3 stack in a controlled environment with known ground-truth closure. An aligned scalar value signal -- the objective at the heart of value equivalence -- installs only a one-dimensional projection of a closure that needs several dimensions: read through a single linear probe, the recoverable structure rises from R^2=0.10 to 0.76 as the scalar is replaced by the full objective. Sweeping the objective's dimensionality from one to four installs exactly that many predictive directions through an auxiliary head, and the same staircase appears -- at attenuated magnitude but the same rank -- through the model's own value head, so the dissociation is dimensional rather than an artifact of head form. Capacity-matched comparisons and in-situ pressure checks rule out the obvious alternatives. The law governs a regime, and we measure its boundary: on a companion closed-loop task whose structure is observable frame by frame, reconstruction installs that structure and the scalar objective suffices -- the objective decides what a latent represents exactly where cheaper training signals cannot already recover it. Value equivalence is thus not all-or-nothing but dimensional: the familiar single-reward objective is its rank-one corner, and a model installs as much of a task's structure as the objective it is asked to predict.

AI.tldr: World model quality depends on objective dimensionality, not just capacity - scalar objectives install only 1D projections of task structure, while higher-dimensional objectives capture more predictive directions.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of world models focuses on reconstruction fidelity or reward prediction as binary quality metrics, but real tasks only need specific predictive coordinates (closure). The paper investigates what determines how much of this closure a latent representation actually captures.
AI.method: Used DreamerV3 in controlled environment with known ground-truth closure. Manipulated objective dimensionality from 1 to 4 dimensions and measured recoverable structure through linear probes. Compared aligned scalar value signals vs. full objectives, capacity-matched comparisons, and in-situ pressure checks to rule out alternatives.
AI.result: Scalar objectives (single-reward) install only 1D projection of closure (R²=0.10), while full objectives capture more dimensions (R²=0.76). Objective dimensionality directly determines how many predictive directions get installed: 1D→1 direction, 4D→4 directions. Same pattern appears in model's value head. On observable tasks, reconstruction can install structure even with scalar objectives.
AI.conclusion: Value equivalence is dimensional, not all-or-nothing. The familiar single-reward objective is rank-one value equivalence. A model installs as much task structure as the dimensionality of the objective it's asked to predict, governing what latent representations capture.

title: SpaCellAgent: A Self-Evolving LLM-Based Multi-Agent Framework for Trajectory Analysis
summary: Spatial and Single-cell transcriptomics are transformative in deciphering cellular dynamics. As the fundamental paradigm for reconstructing cell developmental paths, trajectory inference (TI) is critical. However, existing methods require extensive manual intervention and proficiency in heterogeneous tools, posing a significant barrier to efficient TI analysis. To bridge this gap, we propose SpaCellAgent, an autonomous large language model (LLM) multi-agent framework that automates end-to-end spatiotemporal analysis and narrative generation. SpaCellAgent utilizes a multi-agent architecture for strategic workflow planning, a dynamic tool-orchestration engine for adaptive algorithm selection, and a self-evolution module that iteratively refines performance through feedback. We evaluate SpaCellAgent on six heterogeneous datasets encompassing complex temporal developmental trajectories, diverse sequencing platforms, and spatially-resolved tissue architectures. SpaCellAgent consistently demonstrates over 40\% improvement in analytical efficiency while maintaining expert-aligned performance. By converting natural language specifications into optimized analytical workflows and fully automating the pipeline, SpaCellAgent democratizes advanced spatiotemporal modeling and establishes a scalable, agent-driven paradigm for computational biology. The code and materials are available at https://github.com/LittleXH-shw/SpaCellAgent.

AI.tldr: SpaCellAgent is an autonomous LLM multi-agent framework that automates end-to-end spatiotemporal trajectory inference analysis and narrative generation, achieving over 40% efficiency improvement while maintaining expert-aligned performance.
AI.motivation: Spatial and single-cell transcriptomics are transformative for understanding cellular dynamics, but existing trajectory inference methods require extensive manual intervention and proficiency in heterogeneous tools, creating significant barriers to efficient analysis.
AI.method: SpaCellAgent uses a multi-agent architecture with three key components: 1) strategic workflow planning, 2) dynamic tool-orchestration engine for adaptive algorithm selection, and 3) self-evolution module that iteratively refines performance through feedback. It converts natural language specifications into optimized analytical workflows.
AI.result: The framework was evaluated on six heterogeneous datasets encompassing complex temporal developmental trajectories, diverse sequencing platforms, and spatially-resolved tissue architectures. SpaCellAgent consistently demonstrated over 40% improvement in analytical efficiency while maintaining expert-aligned performance.
AI.conclusion: SpaCellAgent democratizes advanced spatiotemporal modeling by automating trajectory inference analysis, establishing a scalable, agent-driven paradigm for computational biology that converts natural language specifications into optimized analytical workflows.

title: The Approximation Ratio for the Risk of Myopic Bayesian Active Learning for Linear Regression
summary: Active learning studies the fundamental question: what data should we choose to observe? The greedy algorithm in optimal experiment design is a common heuristic and also equivalent to myopic Bayesian active learning for linear regression, the common framework where long-term planning is replaced with the one-step optimal choice. In this work, we prove a first-of-its-kind approximation ratio for the greedy algorithm's risk that is tight up to an absolute constant. The approximation ratio is linear in the maximum initial leverage score (MILS), a newly identified quantity fundamental to the greedy algorithm's performance. Finally, we illustrate the results with simple numerical simulations.

AI.tldr: Greedy algorithm for Bayesian active learning in linear regression achieves tight approximation ratio based on maximum initial leverage score.
AI.motivation: Active learning aims to optimize which data to observe. Greedy algorithm is a common heuristic for optimal experiment design, equivalent to myopic Bayesian active learning for linear regression. However, theoretical guarantees for its performance were lacking.
AI.method: Analysis of greedy algorithm's approximation ratio for risk in Bayesian active learning for linear regression. Introduces maximum initial leverage score (MILS) as key quantity determining greedy algorithm's performance.
AI.result: Proves first-of-its-kind approximation ratio for greedy algorithm's risk that is tight up to an absolute constant. Shows ratio is linear in MILS, providing theoretical foundation for greedy algorithm's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Greedy algorithm for active learning has provable performance guarantees with approximation ratio linearly dependent on MILS. Numerical simulations demonstrate results, establishing theoretical foundation for this common heuristic.

title: Search, Fail, Recover: A Training Framework for Correction-Aware Reasoning
summary: Many reasoning tasks are not well described by a single left-to-right chain: a solver may need to pursue a plausible branch, observe delayed failure, and return to the latest prefix that can still be completed. We introduce Pyligent, a training and inference framework inspired by the Diligent Learner formulation that represents reasoning as validated search over partial solution chains. A task validator labels generated continuations and failures, and the resulting search trees are converted into supervised targets for three actions: continue, finish, and backtrack, with optional traces that summarize abandoned branches. We evaluate Pyligent on a hidden directed graph task designed to isolate delayed-failure recovery, and on structured reasoning domains with exact validators, including $4{\times}4$ Sudoku, Sudoku with reasoning traces, and Blocksworld. Compared with gold-only supervised fine-tuning, Pyligent improves solve rate by $72.7$ percentage points on hidden graphs, by $17$ and $18$ points on mixed and expert Sudoku, by $27$ and $14$ points on mixed and expert Sudoku with reasoning traces, and by $13$ points on Blocksworld. These results suggest that explicit failed-branch supervision can teach useful recovery behavior beyond imitation of polished solution chains.

AI.tldr: Pyligent is a training framework that treats reasoning as search over partial solutions, using validation and backtracking supervision to teach recovery from failed branches, achieving significant improvements on reasoning tasks requiring delayed-failure recovery.
AI.motivation: Many reasoning tasks cannot be solved with simple left-to-right chains; they require exploring plausible branches, handling delayed failures, and backtracking to promising prefixes. Existing methods often only train on successful solutions, failing to teach recovery behavior.
AI.method: Pyligent represents reasoning as validated search over partial solution chains. A task validator labels continuations and failures, converting search trees into supervised targets for three actions: continue, finish, and backtrack (with optional traces of abandoned branches).
AI.result: Pyligent significantly outperforms gold-only supervised fine-tuning: +72.7pp on hidden graphs, +17-18pp on Sudoku, +27-14pp on Sudoku with reasoning traces, and +13pp on Blocksworld. Results show explicit failed-branch supervision teaches useful recovery beyond imitating polished solutions.
AI.conclusion: Explicit supervision of failed branches enables learning effective recovery behavior for complex reasoning tasks, demonstrating that teaching how to recover from mistakes is as important as teaching successful solution patterns.

title: Diffusion enabled Optimal Transport distances for graph matching
summary: This paper introduces Diffusion Semi-Relaxed Fused Gromov-Wasserstein (DsrFGW), a novel method for graph comparison that unifies node features and structural connectivity through optimal transport. While traditional Gromov-Wasserstein and semi-relaxed variants (srGW, srFGW) capture graph structure, they often struggle with sparse, noisy, or partially observed graphs. Inspired by Graph Diffusion Distance, which posits graphs are similar if they enable similar information transmission patterns, DsrFGW incorporates diffusion processes allowing information propagation across nodes, capturing local and global structural patterns while reducing sensitivity to noise or missing edges. An extensive evaluation on 36 synthetic pairwise graph matching tasks (easy, medium, hard) demonstrates consistent superiority over srFGW, achieving accuracy improvements of 0-20 percentage points and dramatic Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) gains: in medium-difficulty scenarios, srFGW often achieves negative ARI (worse than random) while DsrFGW offers better performance in terms of both internal and external clustering quality measures (i.e., Adjusted Rank Index and Accuracy with respect to the true underlying clusters, respectively). Even under severe noise, DsrFGW improves clustering quality in 92% of the synthetic tasks with optimal diffusion scales adapting to problem difficulty, establishing DsrFGW as a robust framework for graph comparison under structural uncertainty.

AI.tldr: DsrFGW is a new graph comparison method that combines optimal transport with diffusion processes to better handle noisy or incomplete graphs by capturing both local and global structural patterns.
AI.motivation: Traditional graph comparison methods like Gromov-Wasserstein and its variants struggle with sparse, noisy, or partially observed graphs. These limitations make them less effective for real-world applications where graph data is often imperfect or incomplete.
AI.method: DsrFGW extends semi-relaxed Fused Gromov-Wasserstein by incorporating diffusion processes that model information propagation across nodes. This allows the method to capture both local and global structural patterns while reducing sensitivity to noise and missing edges. The method adapts diffusion scales to problem difficulty.
AI.result: On 36 synthetic graph matching tasks, DsrFGW consistently outperforms srFGW, achieving 0-20% accuracy improvements. In medium-difficulty scenarios where srFGW often achieved negative ARI (worse than random), DsrFGW provided significantly better performance. Even under severe noise, it improved clustering quality in 92% of tasks.
AI.conclusion: DsrFGW establishes itself as a robust framework for graph comparison under structural uncertainty, effectively unifying node features and structural connectivity through diffusion-enhanced optimal transport that adapts to graph quality and problem difficulty.

title: Do LLM-Generated Skills Make Better AI Data Scientists? A Component Ablation Across Data-Science Workflows
comment: KDD 2026 Workshop on AI Data Scientist
summary: Product data scientists often ask LLM-based agents to help with recurring execution tasks such as cleaning data, writing SQL, choosing statistical tests, and formatting results. Reusable skill files are meant to avoid prompting from scratch by packaging guidance for a task family. Expert-written skills can encode high-quality guidance, but writing and maintaining them across many data-science task families creates a manual bottleneck. We ask whether LLM-generated skills offer a useful low-curation alternative: do they improve performance over the task prompt alone? We test this question across four lifecycle stages: data preparation, data extraction, statistical analysis, and reporting, using one generated skill per stage. We find no reliable improvement from full generated skills over No-Skill prompting. We then ask whether any part of the skill is useful by ablating different skill components. The main ablation covers 56 tasks, nine model configurations, and three providers, yielding 7,560 runs. Compared with prompting using the task alone, neither the full generated skill nor any ablated skill variant significantly improves performance; all p-values are at least 0.396, and the total spread across variants is only 1.2 pp. A supplemental token-matched control adds 1,512 runs and finds that Full skills perform similarly to task-irrelevant skill-formatted content. The results caution against using one LLM-generated skill per data-science workflow as a default single-shot prompting strategy.

AI.tldr: LLM-generated reusable skill files for data science tasks don't reliably improve performance over simple task prompting alone, showing similar results to task-irrelevant content when token-matched.
