AI.method: The proposed LSGD method treats MIONet as a multilinear function with respect to last-layer parameters of branch networks. It uses alternating least squares optimization where each branch network's parameters are optimized sequentially. To handle large system matrices, the method employs Kronecker and Khatri-Rao products along with tensor permutation matrices to factor large matrices into smaller, more manageable ones.
AI.result: The method is designed to be efficient and compatible with general L² loss functions that include regularization terms for each branch network's last layer parameters. It also supports applying linear operators to MIONet outputs within each loss term, making it flexible for various applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LSGD method provides an efficient training approach for MIONets by exploiting their multilinear structure and using tensor factorization techniques to handle computational challenges, generalizing the successful LSGD approach from DeepONets to the more complex MIONet architecture.

title: Physics-guided spatiotemporal neural models for fuel density prediction
summary: This paper presents a physics-guided machine learning (PGML) framework for fuel density prediction, integrating physics constraints and domain knowledge into deep learning models to enhance model accuracy and stability. We explore three deep learning architectures -- ConvLSTM, Adaptive Fourier Neural Operator (AFNONet), and Video Vision Transformer (ViViT) -- to model the spatiotemporal evolution of fuel density. Our approach incorporates differentiable physics-informed terms in the loss function, including a mass-conserving fuel transport term and a rate-of-spread estimation. Experimental results, averaged across multiple independent trials, demonstrate that the proposed PGML framework outperforms purely data-driven baselines without physics constraints in both accuracy and stability. This framework enables computationally efficient, physically plausible fire forecasting to support adaptive prescribed burn management.

AI.tldr: A physics-guided machine learning framework that combines deep learning with physical constraints (mass conservation, spread rate) to predict fuel density for fire forecasting.
AI.motivation: To enhance the accuracy and stability of fuel density predictions for fire forecasting by addressing limitations of purely data-driven approaches, enabling computationally efficient and physically plausible fire forecasting to support adaptive prescribed burn management.
AI.method: Integrated physics constraints and domain knowledge into deep learning models using a PGML framework. Explored three deep learning architectures (ConvLSTM, AFNONet, ViViT) for spatiotemporal modeling of fuel density evolution. Incorporated differentiable physics-informed terms in the loss function including mass-conserving fuel transport and rate-of-spread estimation.
AI.result: The proposed PGML framework outperformed purely data-driven baselines without physics constraints in both accuracy and stability, as demonstrated by experimental results averaged across multiple independent trials.
AI.conclusion: The physics-guided machine learning framework enables computationally efficient, physically plausible fire forecasting, making it valuable for supporting adaptive prescribed burn management strategies.

title: Robust Federated Learning Under Real-World Client Churn
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables training shared models on private, on-device data, but production deployments remain constrained to slow, multi-day refresh cycles due to the complexity of coordinating massive client populations. For applications such as feed ranking, ad targeting, and personalized recommendation, model freshness: the ability to rapidly adapt to new user-local data is critical for maximizing objectives like click-through rate. This lag leaves models stale and unresponsive to volatile data distributions driven by viral trends and shifting user intent. Bridging this gap requires addressing three challenges overlooked by existing FL systems: transient client availability, dynamic data heterogeneity, and delays between model predictions and observable outcomes. We present FeLiX, an FL orchestration framework that minimizes wall-clock time-to-target accuracy on live interaction streams. FeLiX introduces three primitives: (i) streaming-aware availability tiers that leverage lightweight telemetry to identify ready clients at scale; (ii) fresh-utility selection, a dual-tier mechanism that prioritizes statistically valuable updates from devices able to meet tight refresh deadlines; and (iii) informativeness-aware, delay-robust aggregation that incorporates late, high-value updates containing ground-truth outcomes without biasing the global model toward stale distributions. Unlike prior systems that rely on unrealistic oracular knowledge of client availability, FeLiX achieves near-oracular performance in real-world settings. Across CIFAR-10, Google Speech, and realistic low-availability traces, FeLiX reduces wall-clock time-to-target accuracy by up to 2.37X while reducing communication bandwidth by 1.30X compared to state-of-the-art synchronous and asynchronous FL baselines.

AI.tldr: FeLiX is a federated learning orchestration framework that dramatically reduces wall-clock training time by addressing key deployment challenges: transient client availability, dynamic data heterogeneity, and delayed outcome observations, achieving up to 2.37X faster time-to-target accuracy.
AI.motivation: Production FL deployments suffer from slow refresh cycles (multi-day) that leave models stale and unresponsive to volatile data distributions, while applications like feed ranking, ad targeting, and personalized recommendation require rapid adaptation to new user data for maximizing objectives like click-through rate.
AI.method: FeLiX introduces three key primitives: (1) streaming-aware availability tiers using lightweight telemetry to identify ready clients at scale, (2) fresh-utility selection that prioritizes statistically valuable updates from devices meeting tight deadlines, and (3) informativeness-aware, delay-robust aggregation that incorporates late, high-value updates with ground-truth outcomes without biasing toward stale distributions.
AI.result: FeLiX reduces wall-clock time-to-target accuracy by up to 2.37X while reducing communication bandwidth by 1.30X compared to state-of-the-art synchronous and asynchronous FL baselines on CIFAR-10, Google Speech, and realistic low-availability traces, achieving near-oracular performance.
AI.conclusion: FeLiX successfully bridges the gap between FL theory and production deployment by addressing practical challenges of client availability, data heterogeneity, and delayed outcomes, enabling faster model refresh cycles critical for real-world applications requiring rapid adaptation to changing data distributions.

title: Multimodal Spatiotemporal-Frequency Fusion with Peak Enhancement for Cellular Traffic Forecasting
comment: Accepted in the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC)
summary: Accurate forecasting of cellular network traffic is essential for network planning, resource allocation, and quality-of-service assurance in modern mobile communication systems. Real-world traffic often exhibits bursty endogenous dynamics and disturbances triggered by external urban events, which makes reliable prediction highly challenging. Most existing spatiotemporal traffic forecasting methods primarily focus on intrinsic traffic patterns or structural relationships within a single modality, and rarely model burst behavior together with exogenous contextual signals. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{MSPF-Net}, a multimodal cellular traffic forecasting framework that integrates external contextual information. Specifically, MSPF-Net consists of a Spatiotemporal-Frequency Traffic Encoder for capturing temporal, spatial, and spectral traffic patterns, a Peak Enhancement Module for extracting burst-aware representations of sudden spikes, a News Context Representation Module for encoding urban news streams into exogenous contextual embeddings, and a Dynamic Fusion Prediction Module for adaptively integrating these heterogeneous signals to generate forecasts. Experiments on the Milano, Trento, and LTE traffic datasets demonstrate that jointly modeling traffic dynamics, burst patterns, and news contextual signals can effectively improve forecasting performance.

AI.tldr: MSPF-Net is a multimodal framework for cellular traffic forecasting that jointly models traffic dynamics, burst patterns, and external news context to improve prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Cellular network traffic forecasting is challenging due to bursty endogenous dynamics and disturbances from external urban events. Most existing methods focus on intrinsic patterns or structural relationships within single modalities, but rarely model burst behavior together with exogenous contextual signals.
AI.method: MSPF-Net integrates external contextual information through four main components: 1) Spatiotemporal-Frequency Traffic Encoder for temporal, spatial, and spectral patterns; 2) Peak Enhancement Module for burst-aware representations; 3) News Context Representation Module for urban news stream embeddings; 4) Dynamic Fusion Prediction Module for adaptive integration of heterogeneous signals.
AI.result: Experiments on Milano, Trento, and LTE traffic datasets demonstrate that jointly modeling traffic dynamics, burst patterns, and news contextual signals effectively improves forecasting performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MSPF-Net framework successfully addresses the limitations of existing methods by integrating multiple modalities, showing that modeling both endogenous traffic patterns and exogenous contextual information leads to more accurate cellular traffic forecasting.

title: UP: Unbounded Positive Asymmetric Optimization for Breaking the Exploration-Stability Dilemma
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become the standard paradigm for enhancing the complex reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). To achieve sample efficiency, modern RL frameworks rely on importance sampling (IS). However, these algorithms suffer from an exploration-stability dilemma. Pure IS often leads to catastrophic training instability, while standard clipping mechanisms used to mitigate this instability strictly constrain the policy update budget. By formalizing the concept of Probability Capacity (Cap), we reveal that conservative clipping structurally stifles exploration by prematurely truncating the update budget for correct but low-confidence reasoning paths. To break free from these constraints, we propose Unbounded Positive Asymmetric Optimization (UP), a universal and plug-and-play objective. UP theoretically restructures the optimization process by anchoring the policy to its current state via the stop-gradient operator. This asymmetric design unleashes unclipped, stable gradients for positive advantages to maximize exploration, while maintaining standard clipping safeguards for negative advantages to prevent training instability. Furthermore, our formulation readily extends across different optimization granularities, including token-level (GRPO, DAPO) and sequence-level (GSPO) frameworks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UP enhances exploration capacity and achieves superior reasoning accuracy across diverse RL algorithms (DAPO, GSPO, and GRPO), model architectures (Dense, MoE, and vision-language), and training modalities (language and multimodal), validating UP as a truly universal plug-and-play enhancement for RL-based training.

AI.tldr: UP (Unbounded Positive Asymmetric Optimization) is a universal plug-and-play RL objective that breaks the exploration-stability dilemma by allowing unclipped positive updates while maintaining clipping for negative updates.
AI.motivation: Current RL frameworks using importance sampling face an exploration-stability dilemma: pure IS causes training instability, while standard clipping mechanisms constrain policy updates too much, especially for correct but low-confidence reasoning paths.
AI.method: Proposes Unbounded Positive Asymmetric Optimization (UP) which anchors the policy to its current state using stop-gradient operator, creating an asymmetric design: unclipped stable gradients for positive advantages to maximize exploration, while maintaining standard clipping for negative advantages to prevent instability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show UP enhances exploration capacity and achieves superior reasoning accuracy across diverse RL algorithms (DAPO, GSPO, GRPO), model architectures (Dense, MoE, vision-language), and training modalities (language and multimodal).
AI.conclusion: UP serves as a truly universal plug-and-play enhancement for RL-based training of LLMs, breaking free from the constraints of traditional clipping while maintaining stability.

title: Latent graph encoding of multimodal neuroimaging features with generative AI architectures
comment: 6 pages, accepted in IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP) 2026
summary: While generative models enable encoding of complex neuroimaging data for feature generation and reconstruction, developing optimal architectural frameworks with appropriate encoding and latent space processes is crucial for studying structural and functional properties of the brain. We design a multimodal generative framework for structural and functional magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) features through systematic evaluation of encoding strategies, latent multimodal fusion, and generative model selection. Using structural gray matter volume (GMV) and static functional network connectivity (sFNC) features from a large neuroimaging dataset, we analyze generative frameworks involving variational autoencoders (VAEs), transformers, generative adversarial networks (GANs), and diffusion models. Architectures that employ modality-aware graph encoding of functional connectivity into a lower-dimensional latent space outperform vectorized encoders or direct data space approaches. The proposed multimodal graph VAE (gMMVAE) surpasses alternative generative variants across multiple metrics for generation fidelity, reconstruction quality, efficiency, and latent space discriminability, highlighting its potential for robust multimodal neuroimaging analysis.

AI.tldr: A multimodal generative framework for brain MRI analysis using graph variational autoencoders outperforms other methods in generating and reconstructing structural and functional features.
AI.motivation: Generative models can encode complex neuroimaging data, but optimal architectural frameworks with appropriate encoding and latent space processes are needed to study structural and functional brain properties effectively.
AI.method: Systematically evaluated encoding strategies, latent multimodal fusion, and generative model selection. Used structural GMV and functional sFNC features from large dataset. Compared VAEs, transformers, GANs, and diffusion models. Employed modality-aware graph encoding of functional connectivity into lower-dimensional latent space.
AI.result: Modality-aware graph encoding outperforms vectorized encoders or direct data space approaches. The proposed multimodal graph VAE (gMMVAE) surpasses alternative generative variants across multiple metrics for generation fidelity, reconstruction quality, efficiency, and latent space discriminability.
AI.conclusion: The gMMVAE framework shows strong potential for robust multimodal neuroimaging analysis, with graph-based encoding proving superior for capturing complex brain connectivity patterns.

title: Gimitest: A Comprehensive Tool for Testing Reinforcement Learning Policies
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) policies can be unsafe and vulnerable to attacks. Ensuring their reliability is often a pain point as existing automated testing methods target only selected environments, testing scenarios, and RL algorithms. To address this, we propose a comprehensive framework for testing single- and multi-agent RL policies under varying conditions. Our implementation of this framework, Gimitest, is an open-source tool that supports various gym frameworks and allows for modifications of their integrated components. This article describes the framework and details Gimitest's functionality and architecture. It showcases its effectiveness in testing multiple RL policies in environments such as the official Farama Gymnasium and PettingZoo.

AI.tldr: A framework called Gimitest for comprehensive testing of single- and multi-agent reinforcement learning policies under varying conditions, addressing vulnerabilities and reliability issues.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning policies are often unsafe and vulnerable to attacks, and existing automated testing methods are limited - they only target selected environments, testing scenarios, and RL algorithms, creating reliability concerns.
AI.method: Proposed a comprehensive testing framework with implementation called Gimitest, an open-source tool that supports various gym frameworks and allows for modifications of their integrated components. The framework enables testing of RL policies under varying conditions.
AI.result: Showcased effectiveness in testing multiple RL policies in environments such as the official Farama Gymnasium and PettingZoo, demonstrating the tool's capability for comprehensive testing across different scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Gimitest provides a comprehensive solution for testing RL policies that addresses limitations of existing methods, offering flexible testing across multiple environments, scenarios, and algorithms to improve reliability and safety of reinforcement learning systems.

title: Dissociating the Internal Representations of Sycophancy in LLMs
comment: Accepted to Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently exhibit sycophancy, where they agree with a user's statement even when incorrect. While sycophancy is often treated as a single defined behavior, it can manifest in substantially distinct ways and circumstances, raising the question of whether this multi-faceted nature is reflected in its internal mechanisms. To address this gap, we dissociate the representations of sycophancy into factual and opinion subtypes -- motivated by the distinction between verifiable claims and subjective beliefs. We train linear probes and construct steering vectors on activations of one subtype and evaluate their transfer to the other subtype to measure to what extent they share representations. We find evidence that different LLMs represent these subtypes differently, with either more unified or more distinct and causally interfering representations. This method of dissociation offers a promising framework for studying the representational structure of complex model behaviors.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit different types of sycophancy (factual vs. opinion), which have distinct internal representations that can be dissociated and studied.
AI.motivation: Sycophancy in LLMs is often treated as a single behavior, but it manifests in distinct ways (factual vs. opinion-based), raising questions about whether these different types have distinct internal mechanisms.
AI.method: Dissociate sycophancy representations into factual and opinion subtypes, train linear probes and construct steering vectors on one subtype's activations, then evaluate their transfer to the other subtype to measure shared representations.
AI.result: Different LLMs represent these subtypes differently - some have more unified representations while others have more distinct and causally interfering representations for factual vs. opinion sycophancy.
AI.conclusion: This dissociation method provides a promising framework for studying the representational structure of complex model behaviors, revealing that sycophancy is not a monolithic phenomenon but has distinct subtypes with potentially separate mechanisms.

title: Intrinsic Green's Learning: Supervised Learning on Manifolds via Inverse PDE
comment: Accepted at AI & PDE Workshop @ ICLR 2026
summary: We introduce Intrinsic Green's Learning (IGL), a framework that models a target function on a manifold as the solution to a linear PDE whose source term is learned from data. Rather than approximating the target directly, IGL learns a source and integrates it against a Green's kernel. An encoder discovers a low-dimensional coordinate chart on the manifold where both the source and the kernel decompose as low-rank tensors, collapsing a high-dimensional integral into independent one-dimensional integrals with cost linear in the intrinsic dimension. A two-stage algorithm separates coordinate discovery from source fitting, a near-convex linear solve, preventing the dimensional collapse of joint training. Learnable gates on each coordinate automatically discover the intrinsic dimension of the manifold. We validate IGL on synthetic manifolds and on MNIST, where it simultaneously achieves near-optimal classification and automatic recovery of the intrinsic dimension.

AI.tldr: IGL models functions on manifolds by learning PDE source terms rather than direct function approximation, using coordinate charts for computational efficiency and intrinsic dimension discovery.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of learning target functions on manifolds where high dimensionality makes traditional approaches computationally expensive. There's a need to efficiently model functions on manifolds while discovering their intrinsic structure.
AI.method: IGL learns a source term for a linear PDE whose solution is the target function. An encoder discovers low-dimensional coordinate charts where source and Green's kernel decompose as low-rank tensors, reducing high-dimensional integrals to independent 1D integrals. A two-stage algorithm separates coordinate discovery from source fitting with learnable gates to automatically discover intrinsic dimension.
AI.result: IGL achieves computational efficiency with cost linear in intrinsic dimension. On MNIST, it simultaneously achieves near-optimal classification accuracy and automatic recovery of the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold.
AI.conclusion: IGL provides an efficient framework for learning functions on manifolds by leveraging PDE theory and coordinate learning, with automatic intrinsic dimension discovery and computational advantages for high-dimensional data.

title: On the Principles of Deep Feedforward ReLU Networks
summary: The architecture of deep feedforward neural networks is ubiquitous in deep learning, either as a whole system or as a subnetwork of other architectures, and thus its mechanism is a key ingredient of the black box of neural networks. On the basis of the simplest two-layer ReLU network, this paper systematically studies the mechanism of deep feedforward ReLU networks with multiple hidden layers and successfully explains the training solution obtained by the back-propagation algorithm. The concept of a path, especially in terms of the relationships between paths, plays a central role in uncovering the mystery of the black box. It is shown that a unit of a deep ReLU network can form a piecewise linear manifold to divide the input space, instead of a hyperplane of the two-layer case. How to efficiently use the hidden-layer units to produce both linear functions and partitions of the input space is also a central problem. The principles of a two-layer ReLU network can be generalized to the deeper case to a large extent, such as multiple strict partial orders and continuity restriction. The combination of the basic and simple principles proposed can yield complicated instantiations including the training solutions, and in this sense the black box of deep feedforward ReLU networks is revealed.

AI.tldr: Deep ReLU networks function through path relationships and piecewise linear manifolds, with training solutions explained by generalizing principles from two-layer networks to deeper architectures.
AI.motivation: Deep feedforward neural networks are fundamental in deep learning but act as black boxes. Understanding their mechanisms, especially for ReLU networks, is crucial for explaining how training solutions emerge from back-propagation algorithms.
AI.method: Systematic study based on simplest two-layer ReLU networks, analyzing path relationships between units. Focuses on how deep ReLU networks form piecewise linear manifolds to partition input space, extending principles like multiple strict partial orders and continuity restriction to deeper architectures.
AI.result: Shows that deep ReLU network units create piecewise linear manifolds (instead of hyperplanes) to divide input space. Principles from two-layer networks generalize to deeper cases, explaining training solutions obtained via back-propagation through path relationships and partition mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The black box of deep feedforward ReLU networks can be understood through basic principles of path relationships, piecewise linear manifolds, and partition efficiency. Simple principles combine to create complex training solutions, revealing the network's internal mechanisms.

title: Online Data Selection Is Implicit Alignment
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is often treated as a capability-adaptation step, while alignment is attributed to later preference optimization or reinforcement learning. This separation is incomplete: when examples are scored and kept online during fine-tuning, the choice of which data to train on already changes the model's behavioral preferences. We study online data selection as an implicit alignment mechanism. Given the same base model, optimizer, and selected-token budget, we compare random, loss-based, quality-based, and diversity-based online selectors and measure the behavioral drift they induce without any preference optimization. The proposed evaluation tracks helpfulness, refusal rate, verbosity, truthfulness, sycophancy, calibration, and jailbreak robustness, together with diagnostics for which behavioral modes are over-represented in the selected data. We formalize online selection as a reweighted SFT objective whose weights define an implicit preference over response styles and safety postures, so that an online scorer plays the role usually assigned to a reward model. This view predicts that high-scoring data can systematically favor longer, more assertive, more compliant, or more refusal-prone behaviors depending on how the online score is defined. Empirically, selectors that are statistically indistinguishable in task accuracy diverge sharply in refusal rate, verbosity, and sycophancy, and we show that the direction of the shift is predictable from the attribute mixture of the selected data. We introduce Alignment Drift Auditing (ADA), a controlled protocol for quantifying selection-induced behavioral movement, and Alignment-Aware Selection (AAS), a diagnostic online selector that retains data efficiency while constraining drift along safety and style axes.

AI.tldr: Online data selection during SFT acts as an implicit alignment mechanism that induces behavioral shifts without preference optimization, with different selection strategies causing measurable divergence in refusal rates, verbosity, and sycophancy.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the traditional separation between supervised fine-tuning (as capability adaptation) and preference optimization (as alignment), arguing that online data selection during SFT already influences the model's behavioral preferences and constitutes an implicit alignment mechanism.
AI.method: 1. Formalized online selection as a reweighted SFT objective where weights define implicit preferences. 2. Compared different online selectors (random, loss-based, quality-based, diversity-based) with controlled token budgets. 3. Developed Alignment Drift Auditing (ADA) protocol to quantify behavioral drift. 4. Created Alignment-Aware Selection (AAS) for efficient selection while constraining behavioral drift.
AI.result: Selectors statistically indistinguishable in task accuracy diverged sharply in refusal rate, verbosity, and sycophancy. The direction of behavioral shifts was predictable from attribute mixture in selected data. Online scoring effectively functions like a reward model, systematically favoring specific response styles and safety postures.
AI.conclusion: Online data selection during SFT is a significant but often overlooked alignment mechanism that induces measurable behavioral drift. The proposed ADA protocol helps audit this drift, and AAS provides a practical approach to maintain data efficiency while controlling undesirable behavioral shifts.

title: Complexity-Budgeted, Interaction-Aware Interpretable Model for Tabular Data
summary: Inherently interpretable classifiers for tabular data typically rely on sparse features, rules, or patterns that users can inspect directly. The marginal feature-screening step common to these methods can discard variables whose predictive value emerges only through joint configurations with other variables. We present Interaction Aware Interpretable Machine Learning (IAIML), a framework that addresses this limitation through three coordinated mechanisms: adaptive per-feature discretization, finite-grid pairwise interaction scoring, and a partitioned explanation budget. Detected interactions are routed through one of two strategies: relaxing the screening filter so that interaction-supported variables enter the pattern search, or constructing explicit pair terms for a sparse downstream classifier. On a 40-dataset panel comprising 24 real-world tabular benchmarks and 16 synthetic interaction stress tests, evaluated under nested cross-validation, IAIML achieves mean AUC within 1.4 points of tuned gradient-boosted ensembles while requiring roughly 14--28 times fewer fitted explanation components. On datasets with strong pairwise interaction structure and low marginal signal, IAIML outperforms all baselines. Among compact interpretable methods, IAIML is comparable to RuleFit in AUC and component count and is less expensive to tune. EBM obtains a small but significant AUC advantage across the full panel, with a substantially larger lookup-table footprint. Performance degrades on datasets requiring higher-order interactions beyond the pairwise scope. Component-isolated ablations confirm that adaptive discretization and interaction-aware admission each contribute incrementally. These results support IAIML as a compact, interaction-aware framework appropriate for settings where bounded explanation size and controlled treatment of feature interactions are design requirements.

AI.tldr: IAIML is an interpretable ML framework that detects and incorporates pairwise feature interactions, achieving competitive AUC with gradient-boosted ensembles while using 14-28x fewer explanation components, making it suitable for settings requiring compact, interaction-aware models.
AI.motivation: Existing interpretable classifiers for tabular data often use marginal feature screening that discards variables whose predictive value only emerges through joint configurations with other features, missing important interactions.
AI.method: Three coordinated mechanisms: adaptive per-feature discretization, finite-grid pairwise interaction scoring, and partitioned explanation budget. Detected interactions are routed through either relaxed screening filters or explicit pair terms for sparse downstream classifiers.
AI.result: On 40-dataset panel (24 real-world benchmarks + 16 synthetic tests), IAIML achieves mean AUC within 1.4 points of gradient-boosted ensembles while requiring 14-28x fewer explanation components. Outperforms all baselines on datasets with strong pairwise interactions and low marginal signal.
AI.conclusion: IAIML provides a compact, interaction-aware framework suitable for settings requiring bounded explanation size and controlled treatment of feature interactions, though performance degrades with higher-order interactions beyond pairwise scope.

title: Constrained Decoding for Diffusion Language Models via Efficient Inference over Finite Automata
summary: Constrained decoding is essential for serving LLMs, ensuring that generated outputs follow specific structures such as JSON schema-formatted function calls. Existing systems are designed for autoregressive models and assume left-to-right generation, masking out invalid next tokens at each step. Diffusion language models, however, break this assumption: they sample multiple positions simultaneously from a fully-factorized mean-field distribution at each denoising step. In this paper, we present an exact and tractable algorithm for sampling from the constrained mean-field posterior under any constraint expressible as a finite automaton. Viewing finite automata as graphical models, we obtain tractable representations of the constrained distribution that enable efficient inference. The approach guarantees constraint satisfaction by construction, supports both greedy and sampling-based decoding, and is compatible with parallel and block-wise decoding under arbitrary remasking schedules. Applying depth-reduction techniques from arithmetic circuit theory, we further reduce sampling depth from linear to logarithmic in the sequence length. Empirical evaluations on Dream-7B and LLaDA-8B show substantial accuracy gains across various tasks including function calling (xLAM, BFCL), planning (Sudoku, Countdown), text-to-SQL (Spider), and math reasoning (GSM-Symbolic), with little inference overhead relative to unconstrained decoding. For example, on BFCL-Live, our approach improves Dream-7B's greedy decoding accuracy from 63.9% to 71.5%, and stochastic sampling accuracy from 22.3% to 69.0%, where the unconstrained baseline collapses, with under 5% wall-clock overhead.

AI.tldr: An exact and tractable algorithm for constrained decoding in diffusion language models that guarantees constraint satisfaction using finite automata representations, with logarithmic sampling depth and minimal inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing constrained decoding systems are designed for autoregressive models with left-to-right generation, but diffusion language models break this assumption by sampling multiple positions simultaneously from mean-field distributions. There's a need for constraint satisfaction methods compatible with diffusion models' parallel sampling nature.
AI.method: Presents an exact algorithm for sampling from constrained mean-field posterior under constraints expressible as finite automata. Views finite automata as graphical models to obtain tractable representations enabling efficient inference. Uses depth-reduction techniques from arithmetic circuit theory to reduce sampling depth from linear to logarithmic in sequence length.
AI.result: Substantial accuracy gains across various tasks: function calling (xLAM, BFCL), planning (Sudoku, Countdown), text-to-SQL (Spider), and math reasoning (GSM-Symbolic). For example, on BFCL-Live, improves Dream-7B's greedy decoding accuracy from 63.9% to 71.5%, and stochastic sampling accuracy from 22.3% to 69.0% where unconstrained baseline collapses, with under 5% wall-clock overhead.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides exact constraint satisfaction for diffusion language models, supports various decoding strategies (greedy/sampling), works with parallel and block-wise decoding under arbitrary remasking schedules, and achieves significant performance improvements with minimal inference cost.

title: Multiplication Beyond Groups: Stratified Fourier Mechanisms in Transformer Circuits
comment: 29 pages, 15 figures. Spotlight at the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at ICML 2026. First three authors contributed equally. Code at https://github.com/uw-math-ai/interpreting-monoids
summary: Transformers have demonstrated a remarkable ability to learn algorithmic reasoning, yet mechanistic analyses have mostly focused on globally invertible operations such as cyclic addition and group composition. In this work, we investigate how small transformers learn modular integer multiplication over composite moduli, a fundamentally non-invertible operation due to the presence of zero-divisors. We propose the monoid extension: a localized generalization of Group Composition via Representation (GCR) that suggests the learned computation does not rely on a single global representation space. Instead, the model partitions the input space into local hierarchical algebraic regions, where group-like structure survives and Fourier mechanisms can be applied. In transformers trained on square-free modular multiplication, we find that embeddings organize around these regions, attention exhibits class-sensitive routing and low-rank write directions, and local character features explain a large fraction of the model's output logits. Our results suggest that representation-theoretic mechanisms previously identified for group operations can extend beyond groups to more general structures.

AI.tldr: Transformers learn modular multiplication over composite moduli by partitioning input space into local algebraic regions instead of using a single global representation, extending representation-theoretic mechanisms from groups to more general structures.
AI.motivation: Previous mechanistic analyses focused on globally invertible operations like group composition, but modular multiplication over composite moduli is non-invertible due to zero-divisors. The authors investigate how transformers learn such fundamentally non-invertible operations.
AI.method: Proposed monoid extension framework, analyzing transformers trained on square-free modular multiplication. Examined embeddings, attention patterns, and local character features using representation-theoretic mechanisms extending beyond groups.
AI.result: Found that embeddings organize around local hierarchical algebraic regions, attention exhibits class-sensitive routing with low-rank write directions, and local character features explain significant portions of output logits.
AI.conclusion: Transformers solve non-invertible algorithmic operations by partitioning input space into local regions where group-like structure survives, extending representation-theoretic mechanisms previously identified only for group operations to more general algebraic structures.

title: Entropy Pacing Policy Optimization for Multi-Task Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Recent breakthroughs of Reinforcement Learning (RL) have highlighted its potential for complex agentic Large Language Model (LLM) tasks. However, existing efforts largely focus on single-task settings, whereas real-world deployment necessitates a generalist agent capable of solving multiple tasks simultaneously. In this work, we identify a critical yet underexplored phenomenon in multi-task agentic RL: different tasks can exhibit exploration-exploitation pace mismatch. Specifically, easier tasks may converge early to low-entropy policies that hinder learning on harder tasks, while harder tasks can, in turn, push easier tasks back toward high-entropy exploration. This back-and-forth interaction creates inter-task entropy crossovers and frequent entropy spikes. Inspired by this observation, we introduce Entropy Pacing Policy Optimization (EPPO) for multi-task agentic LLMs, which coordinates entropy across tasks to stabilize multi-task optimization. At the core of EPPO is a task-wise dynamic clipping mechanism that replaces the fixed clipping threshold in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a task entropy-aware adaptive bound, tightening updates for over-confident tasks while relaxing them for under-explored ones. Experiments on the multi-task agentic benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed EPPO yields results superior to its counterparts.

AI.tldr: EPPO introduces an entropy pacing mechanism for multi-task RL in agentic LLMs to address exploration-exploitation imbalances across tasks, improving performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world LLM deployment requires generalist agents capable of solving multiple tasks simultaneously, but existing RL approaches focus mainly on single-task settings. The paper identifies a critical problem in multi-task RL: different tasks exhibit exploration-exploitation pace mismatch where easier tasks converge early to low-entropy policies that hinder learning on harder tasks, and harder tasks can push easier tasks back toward high-entropy exploration, causing inter-task entropy crossovers and spikes.
AI.method: The authors propose Entropy Pacing Policy Optimization (EPPO) which coordinates entropy across tasks to stabilize multi-task optimization. At its core is a task-wise dynamic clipping mechanism that replaces the fixed clipping threshold in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a task entropy-aware adaptive bound. This mechanism tightens policy updates for over-confident tasks while relaxing them for under-explored ones.
AI.result: Experiments on multi-task agentic benchmarks demonstrate that EPPO yields results superior to its counterparts, showing improved performance over existing methods in multi-task settings.
AI.conclusion: EPPO effectively addresses the exploration-exploitation pace mismatch in multi-task agentic RL by coordinating entropy across tasks through adaptive clipping, leading to more stable optimization and better performance on multi-task benchmarks.

title: Predicting LLM Safety Before Release by Simulating Deployment
summary: Pre-deployment safety evaluations aim to inform the downstream risks of releasing a new AI model. Yet most evaluations provide limited evidence about how often undesired model behavior will occur in deployment: they generally have insufficient coverage, are unrepresentative, and are generally recognizable as tests. To address these concerns, we study a simple way to simulate a model deployment: starting from de-identified conversations from a previous model deployment, we hold fixed the initial conversation prefix and regenerate the next response using a candidate model. The resulting responses can then both be audited for novel misalignments and used to estimate the prevalence of model misbehavior before deployment. We evaluate deployment simulation across four GPT-5-series deployments, using registered, outcome-blinded predictions for GPT-5.4 and retrospective analyses of three earlier releases. We find that deployment simulation produces informative estimates of post-deployment misbehavior rates and outperforms baselines based on adversarially selected production data; its evaluation-awareness point estimates were also much closer to production traffic than those from traditional evaluations. We also identify the realism of tool resampling as a central challenge for further improving predictions and share results suggesting that this challenge is surmountable even in complex tool-use settings. Finally, we show that deployment simulation can be seeded from public chat datasets and remain informative about production misbehavior rates, suggesting a path for external researchers to run deployment-grounded evaluations without access to private production logs. Overall, deployment simulation helps evaluators forecast how language models will behave in the real world and supports more quantitative assessment of deployment risk.

AI.tldr: Deployment simulation - using real past conversations to test new AI models before release - provides better estimates of actual deployment risks than traditional evaluations.
AI.motivation: Current AI safety evaluations have limitations: insufficient coverage, unrepresentative of real deployment, and easily recognizable as tests, providing limited evidence about actual deployment risks.
AI.method: Simulate model deployment by using de-identified conversations from previous deployments, holding fixed the initial conversation prefix, and regenerating the next response using a candidate model. Responses are then audited for misalignments and used to estimate prevalence of model misbehavior.
AI.result: Deployment simulation produced informative estimates of post-deployment misbehavior rates across four GPT-5-series deployments, outperforming adversarial baseline methods. Evaluation-aware point estimates were much closer to production traffic than traditional evaluations. The method also works with public chat datasets, enabling external researchers to conduct deployment-grounded evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Deployment simulation helps evaluators forecast how language models will behave in real-world deployment and supports more quantitative assessment of deployment risk, though realism of tool resampling remains a challenge to address.

title: Gauge-Invariant Learnable Spectral Positional Encodings for Directed Graphs via Hermitian Block Krylov Subspaces
summary: Spectral positional encodings (PEs) for \emph{directed} graphs face two obstacles: magnetic Laplacians require an $O(n^3)$ Hermitian eigendecomposition per potential, and their complex eigenvectors are defined only up to unitary gauge, which prior work handles with basis-invariant architectures. We propose learnable spectral PEs of the form $h_θ(A_q)\,R$, where $A_q$ is a normalized magnetic operator, $h_θ$ a learnable scalar spectral response, and $R$ a block of random probes. Because the PE is a \emph{matrix function} of the operator, it is gauge-invariant by construction. We compute it in a Hermitian block Krylov subspace from sparse matrix--vector products only, prove that $k = O(\log(1/\varepsilon))$ block steps suffice uniformly over heat--resolvent response families, and give a covering-number argument for why low-dimensional structured families generalize where free per-eigenvalue weights overfit. On a directed SBM whose symmetrization is uninformative by construction, direction-blind PEs stay at chance while magnetic Krylov PEs converge to the exact-eigendecomposition oracle as the depth grows. The same probes yield gauge-invariant pairwise features with $1/\sqrt{s}$ Monte-Carlo error, and the undirected $q{=}0$ case improves heterophilous benchmarks over no-PE and polynomial baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a gauge-invariant spectral positional encoding method for directed graphs using magnetic Laplacians and learnable scalar spectral responses, achieving computational efficiency through block Krylov methods and proving theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing spectral positional encodings for directed graphs face two main problems: (1) magnetic Laplacians require expensive O(n³) Hermitian eigendecomposition per potential, and (2) their complex eigenvectors are only defined up to unitary gauge transformations, requiring basis-invariant architectures to handle.
AI.method: Proposed learnable spectral PEs of form h_θ(A_q)R, where A_q is normalized magnetic operator, h_θ is learnable scalar spectral response, and R is block of random probes. Uses Hermitian block Krylov subspace methods with sparse matrix-vector products only. Proved k = O(log(1/ε)) block steps suffice uniformly over heat-resolvent response families.
AI.result: Method is gauge-invariant by construction as a matrix function. On directed SBM with uninformative symmetrization, direction-blind PEs stay at chance while magnetic Krylov PEs converge to exact-eigendecomposition oracle as depth increases. Same probes yield gauge-invariant pairwise features with 1/√s Monte-Carlo error. Undirected case (q=0) improves heterophilous benchmarks over no-PE and polynomial baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides an efficient, theoretically-grounded approach to spectral positional encodings for directed graphs that avoids expensive eigendecompositions while maintaining gauge invariance and achieving strong empirical performance.

title: ORCAID: Oblique Rule-Based Continuous-Action Interpretation for Deep RL Policies
comment: 33 pages, 8 figures, accompanying source code available at https://gitlab.tuwien.ac.at/ignacio.lopez/ORCAID
summary: Explainability remains a key issue in reinforcement learning (RL). Distilling an interpretable policy from an agent trained in a complex environment is particularly challenging when the action space is continuous. We introduce ORCAID, a novel method for extracting interpretable rule-based policies from RL agents operating in mixed continuous-discrete environments with continuous action spaces. Our main contribution is an efficient oblique decision tree training algorithm that partitions the state space by hyperplanes and fits local linear models. The key idea lies in a three-stage split search: efficient random initialization, local refinement, and backward elimination. Finally, adjacent leaves are merged to yield a concise set of interpretable rules describing a given deep RL policy. We evaluate ORCAID across multiple RL environments, demonstrating that the extracted rule-based policies maintain strong performance with a low number of parameters and can even be used to improve the performance of the original deep RL policy.

AI.tldr: ORCAID extracts interpretable rule-based policies from RL agents in mixed continuous-discrete environments with continuous action spaces using oblique decision trees with local linear models.
AI.motivation: Explainability remains a key challenge in reinforcement learning, particularly when dealing with continuous action spaces in complex environments. There's a need to distill interpretable policies from trained RL agents to understand their decision-making processes.
AI.method: ORCAID uses an efficient oblique decision tree training algorithm that partitions state space with hyperplanes and fits local linear models. The algorithm employs a three-stage split search: 1) efficient random initialization, 2) local refinement, and 3) backward elimination. Finally, adjacent leaves are merged to create concise interpretable rules.
AI.result: ORCAID was evaluated across multiple RL environments. The extracted rule-based policies maintained strong performance with low parameter counts and could even improve the performance of the original deep RL policy.
AI.conclusion: ORCAID successfully addresses the explainability challenge in RL with continuous action spaces by extracting interpretable rule-based policies that preserve performance while providing transparency into the agent's decision-making process.

title: FMMVCC: Fuzzy Mamba-based Multi-View Contrastive Clustering for Univariate Time Series
summary: In many realistic scenarios, large volumes of time series data are generated with limited or expensive annotations. This limitation makes supervised learning methods difficult to apply and leads to the use of unsupervised approaches capable of discovering meaningful structures directly from raw data. Clustering therefore plays a crucial role in organizing time series into groups that share similar temporal patterns, enabling exploratory analysis and downstream tasks without requiring manual labeling. However, existing deep clustering methods often struggle to capture long-range temporal dependencies or rely on architectures with high computational cost. This paper introduces FMMVCC, a Mamba-based deep clustering framework for time series that leverages state space sequence modeling to efficiently learn temporal representations with linear complexity. Additionally, it utilizes multi-view self-supervised learning with temporal masking and augmentations. Experimental evaluation in 15 benchmark datasets proves that FMMVCC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving the best overall performance in 29 of 60 total metric evaluations and the highest average rank in all tested scenarios.

AI.tldr: FMMVCC: A Mamba-based deep clustering framework for time series that uses state space modeling with linear complexity and multi-view self-supervised learning, outperforming SOTA methods on 15 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In realistic scenarios, time series data often has limited or expensive annotations, making supervised learning difficult. Clustering is crucial for organizing time series into groups with similar patterns, but existing deep clustering methods struggle with long-range temporal dependencies or have high computational costs.
AI.method: FMMVCC uses Mamba-based state space sequence modeling to efficiently learn temporal representations with linear complexity. It incorporates multi-view self-supervised learning with temporal masking and augmentations for robust feature learning.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on 15 benchmark datasets shows FMMVCC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving best overall performance in 29 out of 60 metric evaluations and the highest average rank in all tested scenarios.
AI.conclusion: FMMVCC provides an effective and efficient deep clustering framework for time series that addresses limitations of existing methods, offering superior performance with linear complexity for handling long-range dependencies in time series data.

title: FedCVESA: Taking Away Training Data in Federated Learning via Correlation Value Encoding and Segmented Aggregation
summary: Federated learning (FL) avoids explicit data exposure by keeping raw data on local clients, yet privacy risks remain in the training process and the learned model itself. Recently, centralized Taking Away Training Data (TATD) attacks have shown that malicious training could abuse the memorization capacity of deep models to store and later recover training data. However, this memorization-based threat has not been systematically studied under FL environments, where multi-client averaging could overwrite encoded training data. In this paper, we study a white-box TATD attack in which a malicious server selects n target clients from K participating clients and actively writes private training data into the global model during federated training. We propose FedCVESA, a federated variant of Correlation Value Encoding Attack (CVEA), by adding a Pearson-correlation regularizer to the loss function of target clients, so that private training data are gradually encoded into selected model parameters, referred to as carrier parameters. To reduce the overwriting of carrier parameters during server aggregation, we further propose segmented aggregation over dispersed carrier parameters, preserving selected carrier parameters while keeping standard averaging on the remaining parameters. Experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 under Dirichlet non-IID partitions show that the proposed method can steal semantically meaningful private training images from the trained model while maintaining acceptable main-task utility in a controlled proof-of-concept setting. These results demonstrate that FL can become a parameter-level memorization channel for active TATD attack under the studied white-box malicious-server setting.

AI.tldr: FedCVESA enables a white-box TATD attack in federated learning where a malicious server actively encodes and steals private training data from target clients through correlation-based parameter manipulation during FL training.
AI.motivation: Federated learning keeps raw data local but still faces privacy risks. While centralized TATD attacks exploit model memorization, the threat hasn't been systematically studied in FL where multi-client averaging could overwrite encoded data. This paper investigates whether FL can become a parameter-level memorization channel for active TATD attacks.
AI.method: Proposes FedCVESA, a federated variant of Correlation Value Encoding Attack (CVEA), where a malicious server selects target clients and adds a Pearson-correlation regularizer to their loss functions to gradually encode private training data into selected carrier parameters. Uses segmented aggregation to preserve encoded data by keeping carrier parameters separate during server aggregation while standard averaging other parameters.
AI.result: Experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 under Dirichlet non-IID partitions show FedCVESA can steal semantically meaningful private training images while maintaining acceptable main-task utility. Demonstrates FL can serve as a parameter-level memorization channel for active TATD attacks in white-box malicious-server settings.
AI.conclusion: Federated learning is vulnerable to active TATD attacks through parameter-level memorization, even with multi-client averaging. FedCVESA effectively demonstrates that FL privacy assurances are limited against white-box malicious-server threats, highlighting the need for stronger defensive mechanisms.

title: Hypergraph Neural Stochastic Diffusion: An SDE Framework for Uncertainty Estimation
summary: Hypergraph neural networks have shown powerful capability in modeling higher-order relations, yet their predictive uncertainty remains underexplored. Unlike pairwise graphs, uncertainty in hypergraphs arises not only from noisy attributes and ambiguous labels, but also from variations in node-hyperedge incidence structures and complex higher-order dependencies. Existing approaches mainly estimate uncertainty from final predictions or rely on computationally expensive ensembles and Bayesian inference, limiting their ability to capture uncertainty evolution during representation learning. In this paper, we propose Hypergraph Neural Stochastic Diffusion(HyperNSD), a stochastic differential equation framework for uncertainty estimation on hypergraphs. HyperNSD models hypergraph representations as stochastic processes evolving over node-hyperedge incidence structures. A learnable drift function captures deterministic higher-order diffusion dynamics, while a learnable stochastic forcing function characterizes structural ambiguity and representation noise. Predictive uncertainty is directly quantified through the variability of stochastic representation trajectories, providing an intrinsic uncertainty measure beyond post-hoc confidence scores. We formulate HyperNSD with neural drift and diffusion networks, enabling joint learning of prediction and uncertainty propagation. Theoretical analyses establish well posedness, perturbation stability,permutation equivariance, and numerical convergence of the proposed stochastic dynamics. Experiments on multiple hypergraph benchmarks demonstrate that HyperNSD achieves reliable uncertainty estimation for out-of-distribution and misclassification detection while preserving competitive prediction accuracy. These results provide a principled stochastic-dynamical framework for trustworthy higher-order representation learning.

AI.tldr: HyperNSD is a stochastic differential equation framework for uncertainty estimation in hypergraph neural networks, modeling representations as stochastic processes over node-hyperedge incidence structures to capture uncertainty evolution during learning.
AI.motivation: Hypergraph neural networks lack exploration of predictive uncertainty, which arises from noisy attributes, ambiguous labels, node-hyperedge incidence variations, and complex higher-order dependencies. Existing uncertainty estimation methods are limited to final predictions or use computationally expensive ensembles/Bayesian inference without capturing uncertainty evolution during representation learning.
AI.method: HyperNSD models hypergraph representations as stochastic processes evolving over node-hyperedge incidence structures. It uses a learnable drift function for deterministic higher-order diffusion dynamics and a learnable stochastic forcing function to characterize structural ambiguity and representation noise. Predictive uncertainty is quantified through stochastic representation trajectory variability, implemented with neural drift and diffusion networks for joint learning of prediction and uncertainty propagation.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple hypergraph benchmarks show HyperNSD achieves reliable uncertainty estimation for out-of-distribution and misclassification detection while maintaining competitive prediction accuracy. The framework also provides theoretical guarantees including well-posedness, perturbation stability, permutation equivariance, and numerical convergence.
AI.conclusion: HyperNSD provides a principled stochastic-dynamical framework for trustworthy higher-order representation learning, directly quantifying predictive uncertainty through stochastic representation trajectories rather than post-hoc confidence scores, addressing limitations of existing hypergraph uncertainty estimation approaches.

title: Latency-Aware Bid Acceptance under Operational Feasibility: A Public Benchmark with Hindsight Ceilings
comment: 20 pages. Benchmark, code, and run manifests: https://github.com/aswincsekar/freightbidbench
summary: Online truckload bid acceptance is a closed-loop stochastic decision problem in which a carrier or broker must, in real time, accept or reject a tendered load subject to operational feasibility, fleet repositioning costs, and opportunity cost against future demand. Public, reproducible benchmarks for this problem are scarce: existing routing benchmarks are static, while dynamic-fleet studies typically rely on private operator data. We introduce FreightBidBench, a public-calibrated, dependency-free, closed-loop benchmark in which feasibility (pickup reach, appointment windows, simplified hours-of-service, stochastic yard delays) and economics (service-failure penalty, terminal fleet value, daily price-premium window) are explicit, versioned, and reproducible from public Freight Analysis Framework and U.S. Department of Agriculture truck rate data. We develop two full-horizon hindsight ceilings: a simple LP style relaxation and a tighter Lagrangian-per-truck information relaxation that retains per-truck hours-of-service and sequencing structure and is 20.7% tighter than the LP relaxation on a tight-capacity scenario and 39.3% tighter on a scarce-capacity scenario. We introduce a parametric surrogate-rollout cascade with boundary-band and scarcity-pressure escalation triggers. On ten-seed tight and scarce scenarios, the best simple policy retains 91.0% and 86.5% of rollout profit and the standard-library surrogate 94.2% and 89.3%; a cascade at a single escalation band recovers roughly 98% on both at 40-56% of rollout's mean decision latency, and on the tight scenario is statistically indistinguishable from the rollout teacher (paired-bootstrap 95% CI on the profit delta spans zero).

AI.tldr: FreightBidBench: a public, reproducible benchmark for online truckload bid acceptance with explicit feasibility constraints and calibrated economics, featuring new relaxation methods and surrogate-rollout policies.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of public, reproducible benchmarks for online truckload bid acceptance problems. Existing benchmarks are either static (routing benchmarks) or rely on private operator data (dynamic fleet studies), making research comparisons difficult.
AI.method: 1) Created FreightBidBench benchmark using public Freight Analysis Framework and USDA truck rate data with explicit feasibility constraints and economics. 2) Developed two full-horizon hindsight ceilings: LP relaxation and tighter Lagrangian-per-truck information relaxation. 3) Introduced parametric surrogate-rollout cascade with boundary-band and scarcity-pressure escalation triggers.
AI.result: 1) Lagrangian-per-truck relaxation was 20.7% tighter than LP relaxation on tight-capacity scenario and 39.3% tighter on scarce-capacity scenario. 2) Best simple policy retained 91.0% and 86.5% of rollout profit on tight/scarce scenarios; standard-library surrogate retained 94.2% and 89.3%. 3) Cascade at single escalation band recovered ~98% profit at 40-56% of rollout's mean decision latency, and was statistically indistinguishable from rollout teacher on tight scenario.
AI.conclusion: FreightBidBench provides a valuable public benchmark for online truckload bid acceptance research, and the proposed surrogate-rollout cascade approach achieves near-optimal performance with significantly reduced computational latency.

title: An Hybrid Quantum-Classical Diffusion Model for Image Generation
summary: Quantum diffusion models provide a physics-consistent route to generative learning by formulating noising and denoising directly on quantum states. However, applying such models to classical high-dimensional data is constrained by the qubit cost of state encoding and the computational burden of simulating large density operators. We propose a scalable hybrid generative pipeline that combines a classical autoencoder for dimensionality reduction with a mixed-state quantum denoising diffusion probabilistic model (MSQuDDPM) operating in the learned latent space. The autoencoder compresses data into compact latent codes that can be embedded into a small-qubit Hilbert space, after which the quantum diffusion model learns a generative distribution over latent density operators and decodes samples back to the original domain. Algorithmically, we simplify the reverse dynamics by predicting an estimate of the clean state $ρ_0$ at timestep $t$ and computing the one-step reverse update via an analytic backward propagation rule, rather than learning an explicit predictor for $ρ_{t-1}$. We demonstrate the proposed approach on MNIST image generation and discuss how mixed-state quantum diffusion can serve as a practical backbone for hybrid quantum--classical generative modeling under realistic qubit budgets.

AI.tldr: Hybrid quantum-classical generative pipeline combining classical autoencoder with mixed-state quantum diffusion model for scalable quantum generative learning on classical data.
AI.motivation: Quantum diffusion models offer physics-consistent generative learning but face constraints: high qubit costs for state encoding and computational burden of simulating large density operators for classical high-dimensional data.
AI.method: 1) Classical autoencoder compresses data into compact latent codes, 2) Mixed-state quantum denoising diffusion probabilistic model (MSQuDDPM) learns generative distribution over latent density operators in small-qubit Hilbert space, 3) Algorithmic simplification: predicts clean state estimate at timestep t and computes one-step reverse update via analytic backward propagation rule instead of learning explicit predictor.
AI.result: Demonstrated on MNIST image generation, showing quantum diffusion can serve as practical backbone for hybrid quantum-classical generative modeling under realistic qubit budgets.
AI.conclusion: Proposed scalable hybrid pipeline enables practical quantum generative modeling for classical data by addressing qubit and computational constraints through latent space compression and algorithmic efficiency.

title: Structural Adversarial Attacks on Relational Deep Learning under Integrity Constraints
comment: To be presented at the VLDB Workshop AI for Databases
summary: Relational Deep Learning (RDL) has become a standard methodology for machine learning on relational databases: the database is encoded as a heterogeneous temporal graph in which tuples become nodes and primary-key to foreign-key (PK-FK) dependencies become typed edges, over which a graph neural network is trained for downstream prediction. We study the adversarial robustness of this pipeline. We consider a white-box attacker who knows how the graph is built and the model is trained, reasons about perturbations on the graph, but can only act on the upstream database, by rewiring foreign-key references while preserving the integrity constraints of the schema (foreign-key validity, the degree-one FK constraint, and functional dependencies). This restricts the attacker to a constrained, combinatorial set of admissible edits under a global perturbation budget, which is intractable to explore exhaustively and made non-additive by GNN message passing. We investigate seven attack heuristics - two random sampling baselines and five gradient-guided variants that exploit differentiable edge masks - and evaluate them on the RelBench rel-f1 benchmark. Gradient-based attacks consistently outperform random baselines on regression tasks, whereas gains on classification are smaller, which we attribute to low label-flip rates and greater local stability of classification outputs.

AI.tldr: Adversarial robustness study of Relational Deep Learning systems where attackers manipulate foreign-key references in databases to poison graph neural networks, with gradient-based attacks outperforming random baselines on regression tasks.
AI.motivation: Relational Deep Learning has become standard for ML on relational databases, but its adversarial robustness is unexplored. The paper investigates how attackers can manipulate database foreign-key references to poison graph neural networks used in these pipelines.
AI.method: White-box attack framework where attacker knows graph construction and model training. Attacker can only rewire foreign-key references while preserving schema integrity constraints. Investigated 7 attack heuristics: 2 random sampling baselines and 5 gradient-guided variants using differentiable edge masks, evaluated on RelBench rel-f1 benchmark.
AI.result: Gradient-based attacks consistently outperform random baselines on regression tasks. Gains on classification tasks are smaller due to low label-flip rates and greater local stability of classification outputs.
AI.conclusion: Relational Deep Learning systems are vulnerable to adversarial attacks through foreign-key rewiring while preserving schema constraints. Gradient-guided attacks are more effective than random attacks, particularly for regression tasks, highlighting the need for robust RDL defenses.

title: On Adversarial Vulnerability of Vision-Language Models through the Lens of Intermediate Spectral Subspaces
summary: Adversarial vulnerability in deep neural networks (DNNs) has been studied from the perspectives of decision-boundary geometry, feature robustness, input-output Jacobians, and the instability of inverse problems. Here, we focus on the spectral structure of intermediate linear transformations that propagate information through modern DNNs, an unexplored mechanism of adversarial vulnerability. Specifically, we investigate transformer-based vision-language models, whose linear layers admit interpretable spectral decompositions and whose widespread adoption makes understanding their robustness increasingly important. We propose a white-box spectral-subspace-guided attack (SSGRA) that aligns intermediate representations with the subspace spanned by the bottom right singular vectors. Our experiments show improved attack effectiveness over existing baselines. In addition, SSGRA offers a spectral interpretation of adversarial vulnerability in VLMs, providing insights for improving their robustness.

AI.tldr: A spectral-subspace-guided attack (SSGRA) targets transformer-based vision-language models by aligning intermediate representations with bottom right singular vectors, improving attack effectiveness and providing spectral insights into adversarial vulnerability.
AI.motivation: Previous studies examined adversarial vulnerability in DNNs through decision-boundary geometry, feature robustness, Jacobians, and inverse problem instability, but the spectral structure of intermediate linear transformations in transformer-based vision-language models remains unexplored despite their widespread adoption and importance for robustness understanding.
AI.method: Proposes SSGRA, a white-box attack that aligns intermediate representations with the subspace spanned by bottom right singular vectors of linear layers in transformer-based vision-language models, leveraging their interpretable spectral decompositions.
AI.result: Experiments show improved attack effectiveness over existing baselines, demonstrating that targeting spectral subspaces is more effective than previous approaches. The method also provides spectral interpretation of adversarial vulnerability in vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: The spectral structure of intermediate linear transformations represents a previously unexplored mechanism of adversarial vulnerability in vision-language models, offering both improved attack capabilities and valuable insights for developing more robust models through spectral analysis.

title: A knowledge-augmented dataset of high-risk driving scenarios with LLM annotations for autonomous driving
summary: Safe autonomous driving requires both rapid responses to common high-risk events and deeper reasoning over rare, extreme long-tail scenarios in traffic safety. These scenarios are severely under-represented in naturalistic driving data, and existing trajectory and language-augmented datasets seldom provide high-risk event labels, semantic annotations, and verifiable safety signals. Here we present K-Risk, a knowledge-augmented dataset that combines structured driving trajectories with large language model generated semantic annotations for safety-critical driving scenarios. K-Risk integrates 20 human-driven and autonomous-vehicle trajectory datasets from Europe, China, and the United States, covering highways, urban freeways, intersections, and roundabouts. Using a unified risk-centric extraction pipeline, K-Risk curates 31,398 high-risk events, together with a 1,036-event extreme subset of near-collision cases. Each event is released as a synchronized trajectory, metadata, and language triplet containing structured scenario descriptions, abnormal-behavior notifications, and, for a representative subset, causal risk analyses and action recommendations validated through a closed-loop simulator with iterative reflection. By combining multi-dimensional risk annotations, interpretable language supervision, and verifiable decisions, K-Risk bridges structured traffic trajectories, semantic reasoning, and decision supervision, providing a standardized foundation for developing and evaluating next-generation risk-aware autonomous driving agents.

AI.tldr: K-Risk is a knowledge-augmented dataset that combines structured driving trajectories with LLM-generated semantic annotations for safety-critical scenarios, addressing long-tail safety events in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets lack sufficient representation of rare, extreme long-tail safety scenarios in autonomous driving, and current trajectory/language datasets often miss high-risk event labels, semantic annotations, and verifiable safety signals needed for robust autonomous system development.
AI.method: The authors created K-Risk by integrating 20 human-driven and autonomous vehicle trajectory datasets from multiple regions (Europe, China, US) and road types. They used a unified risk-centric extraction pipeline to curate high-risk events, combined with large language model generated semantic annotations, and validated recommendations through closed-loop simulation with iterative reflection.
AI.result: K-Risk contains 31,398 high-risk events (including 1,036 extreme near-collision cases) released as synchronized trajectory-metadata-language triplets. Each event includes structured scenario descriptions, abnormal-behavior notifications, causal risk analyses, and action recommendations that are simulator-validated.
AI.conclusion: K-Risk bridges structured traffic trajectories, semantic reasoning, and decision supervision, providing a standardized foundation for developing and evaluating next-generation risk-aware autonomous driving agents through multi-dimensional risk annotations and verifiable decisions.

title: RLVP: Penalize the Path, Reward the Outcome
summary:
Agents acting on our behalf in the real world (e.g. placing phone calls) must learn online from costly, often irreversible interactions rather than cheap simulator steps. Two things follow. First, deployability depends on the path, not only the outcome. An agent must respect outcome-neutral constraints such as not repeatedly calling an unresponsive user, respecting business hours, or completing required authentication constraints that outcome-based rewards cannot express, since violating them frequently improves apparent success. Second, because each interaction is expensive, the agent must learn efficiently from very few examples.
  Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) is blind to both challenges: it optimizes solely on the outcome and wastes expensive rollouts on all-fail groups where group-relative advantage collapses to zero. Attempts to densify supervision by rewarding progress target the hard-to-verify direction. In contrast, real agentic environments can cheaply detect bad moves. Since group-relative advantage is equivalent to within-group variance, a dense signal helps only when it supplies variance the outcome lacks. A verifiable penalty on the path meets this condition reliably, while a progress potential helps only where partial progress is reachable. The resulting recipe "penalize the path, reward the outcome" achieves high task success with near-zero violations, where outcome-only training violates constraints on nearly every episode. We provide four design rules for effective penalties, including avoidance of the inaction trap that arises when a penalty is used in isolation.

AI.tldr: RLVR framework for real-world agents that combines path penalties with outcome rewards to enable constraint-respecting learning from few expensive interactions
AI.motivation: Real-world agents (e.g., phone call systems) face two key challenges: 1) They must respect outcome-neutral constraints (business hours, authentication rules) that pure outcome-based rewards can't express, 2) They must learn efficiently from very few expensive interactions since real-world rollouts are costly and irreversible
AI.method: Proposes "penalize the path, reward the outcome" approach where verifiable penalties are applied to constraint-violating actions during the execution path, while rewarding only successful outcomes. Four design rules are provided for effective penalty implementation, including avoiding the inaction trap when penalties are used alone
AI.result: The approach achieves high task success with near-zero constraint violations, whereas outcome-only training violates constraints on nearly every episode. Path penalties provide reliable dense signals for learning when group-relative advantage would otherwise collapse to zero
AI.conclusion: Traditional RLVR is insufficient for real-world agents as it ignores path constraints and wastes expensive interactions. The proposed method of combining verifiable path penalties with outcome rewards enables deployable agents that respect real-world constraints while learning efficiently from few examples

title: Distributed Sparse Interventions in Language Models
summary: Language models perform a wide range of tasks at varying levels of abstraction with the capacity to flexibly infer tasks from context, execute multiple tasks simultaneously, and select among competing tasks. To study the role of model components in task behaviour, their causal influence can be investigated through interventions. Prior work on model steering has largely focused on interventions along global directions in activation space, modeling task representations as approximately linear and additive. By studying interventions at the neuron level, we find substantial, neuron-specific nonlinear effects on model outputs that are not captured by current steering approaches.
