CySecBERT performs best overall, with significant gains in multi-label setting. Confusion patterns follow CWE hierarchy (Pearson r > 0.92 across encoders). Hierarchy-relaxed evaluation raises macro-F1 from ~81% to ~90%, showing strict metrics understate actual classifier quality.
AI.conclusion: The error structure in CWE classification is driven more by taxonomy design than encoder choice. Multi-class formulation generally outperforms multi-label, but post-hoc optimization can close gaps for smaller label spaces. Hierarchy-relaxed evaluation provides better assessment of branch-level classification quality, suggesting future work should consider hierarchical relationships in CWE classification tasks.

title: Asymmetric Focal Loss Improves Graph Neural Network Prediction of Drug-Drug Interactions
summary: Background: Graph neural networks improve computational prediction of polypharmacy side effects, but standard binary cross-entropy training allocates equal capacity to well-classified and difficult examples, potentially missing clinically significant interactions. We evaluated whether an asymmetric focal objective could improve multi-relational drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction by emphasizing difficult positive interactions. Methods: ClinicalFocal loss was integrated into a relation-aware graph convolutional network using molecular fingerprints, physicochemical descriptors, and learned embeddings. The model was evaluated on TWOSIDES using five-fold cross-validation with identical experimental conditions (architecture, features, data partitions, hyperparameters, and random seeds) for ClinicalFocal loss and binary cross-entropy baseline. Results: ClinicalFocal loss increased accuracy from 0.699 to 0.892 (+19.3 percentage points) and F1 score from 0.700 to 0.894 (+19.4 percentage points). AUROC increased from 0.766 to 0.914, and AUCPR increased from 0.714 to 0.860. The false-negative rate decreased from 29.8% to 9.1%, while specificity increased from 69.6% to 87.5%. Overall classification error decreased from 30.1% to 10.8%, corresponding to a 64.1% relative reduction. Improvements were consistent across all five folds. Conclusions: Asymmetric focal optimization improved classification and ranking performance while achieving 90.9% recall for observed interaction triples, without modifying the underlying architecture. Loss-function design is a direct, tunable lever for improving graph-based DDI prediction.

AI.tldr: Asymmetric focal loss improves drug-drug interaction prediction by emphasizing difficult positive cases, boosting accuracy by 19.3 percentage points without architecture changes.
AI.motivation: Standard binary cross-entropy training allocates equal capacity to well-classified and difficult examples, potentially missing clinically significant drug-drug interactions that are important for patient safety.
AI.method: Integrated ClinicalFocal loss into relation-aware graph convolutional network using molecular fingerprints, physicochemical descriptors, and learned embeddings. Evaluated on TWOSIDES dataset with five-fold cross-validation using identical experimental conditions for both loss functions.
AI.result: ClinicalFocal loss increased accuracy from 0.699 to 0.892 (+19.3 percentage points), F1 score from 0.700 to 0.894 (+19.4 percentage points), AUROC from 0.766 to 0.914, and AUCPR from 0.714 to 0.860. False-negative rate decreased from 29.8% to 9.1%, with overall classification error reduced by 64.1%.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetric focal optimization significantly improves classification and ranking performance while achieving 90.9% recall for observed interactions. Loss-function design is a direct, tunable lever for improving graph-based drug-drug interaction prediction without modifying underlying architecture.

title: Higher-Order Geometric Updates for Levenberg-Marquardt Method via Riemann Normal Coordinates
summary: Nonlinear least-squares optimization is central to regression, physics-informed neural networks, and other machine-learning tasks. Such problems have a natural geometric interpretation, model predictions form a manifold in data space, while the chosen parameterization can introduce parameter-effects curvature that becomes a dominant source of nonlinearity. This exposes a limitation of the Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) method, its tangent-space step is applied as a straight update in parameter coordinates. Geodesic acceleration gives a second-order correction, but its removal of parameter-effect curvature is exact only in the infinitesimal-step limit. We propose a Riemann-normal-coordinate Levenberg-Marquardt method (RNC-LM) to improve this consistency for finite optimization steps. By reformulating the geodesic equation, RNC-LM extends geodesic acceleration to arbitrary-order corrections and constructs finite-step updates with progressively higher reparameterization consistency. A line search along the resulting RNC curve controls the traveled distance while keeping the cost close to standard LM. The method eliminates the tangential component of residual acceleration order by order in a moving tangent frame, making the actual objective reduction more consistent with the linear model prediction of LM. On classical nonlinear least-squares benchmarks, RNC-LM improves convergence and robustness in curved valleys and rank-deficient problems. On a reaction-diffusion PINN failure-mode benchmark, it reduces the relative L2 error to the order of 1e-3 and recovers a physically meaningful solution. On a large-scale machine-learning potential-energy-surface fitting task, it achieves a 34-fold speedup over standard LM.

AI.tldr: RNC-LM extends Levenberg-Marquardt using Riemann normal coordinates to remove parameter-effect curvature more consistently for finite optimization steps, improving convergence in curved problems and accelerating large-scale tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard Levenberg-Marquardt has limitations in handling parameter-effects curvature - its tangent-space update remains in parameter coordinates, and geodesic acceleration's correction is only exact in infinitesimal steps.
AI.method: Proposes Riemann-normal-coordinate Levenberg-Marquardt (RNC-LM) that reformulates the geodesic equation to extend geodesic acceleration to arbitrary-order corrections, constructing finite-step updates with higher reparameterization consistency via line search along RNC curves.
AI.result: On nonlinear least-squares benchmarks, RNC-LM improves convergence and robustness in curved valleys and rank-deficient problems. On PINN benchmarks, reduces relative L2 error to ~1e-3 and recovers physically meaningful solutions. On large-scale ML potential fitting, achieves 34× speedup over standard LM.
AI.conclusion: RNC-LM successfully addresses LM's parameter-effects curvature limitations by using Riemann normal coordinates for more consistent finite-step optimization, demonstrating practical benefits across regression, PINNs, and large-scale machine learning tasks.

title: An optimal control approach for neural network architecture adaptation with a posteriori error estimation
summary: This work presents a novel approach for adapting neural network architecture along the depth based on a posteriori error estimation. By formulating neural network training as a continuous-time optimal control problem, we derive rigorous error estimates that quantify how approximation error distributes across network layers. This error decomposition enables a principled depth adaptation strategy: new layers are inserted at locations of maximum estimated error, allowing the network to efficiently capture complex, nonlinear variations in the underlying problem. Our framework introduces a novel network architecture that treats weights and biases as piecewise linear functions varying across layers, with the error estimator bounding the discrepancy between this discrete representation and the true continuous optimal control solution. The approach leverages dual weighted residual methodology from finite element analysis to derive computable upper bounds on the functional error. A key theoretical contribution is the derivation of explicit error bounds that decompose the total approximation error into interval-wise contributions, providing a rigorous basis for targeted architecture refinement. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on scientific datasets, including learning the observable-to-parameter map for the Navier-Stokes equation. Numerical results reveal that our approach consistently outperforms existing architecture adaptation methods in terms of generalization performance.

AI.tldr: A novel neural network depth adaptation method using a posteriori error estimation based on optimal control formulation, enabling targeted layer insertion where error is highest for improved performance.
AI.motivation: Current neural network architectures are often designed heuristically without rigorous guidance on optimal depth or layer placement. The paper aims to develop a principled, mathematically rigorous approach to dynamically adapt network architecture based on error analysis.
AI.method: Formulates neural network training as a continuous-time optimal control problem, derives rigorous error estimates, and introduces dual weighted residual methodology from finite element analysis. Treats weights/biases as piecewise linear functions across layers and uses error estimator to bound discrepancy between discrete representation and true continuous solution.
AI.result: Derives explicit error bounds decomposing total approximation error into interval-wise contributions. Demonstrates effectiveness on scientific datasets including learning observable-to-parameter map for Navier-Stokes equation. Consistently outperforms existing architecture adaptation methods in generalization performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a mathematically rigorous, error-driven approach to neural network depth adaptation that improves generalization by strategically inserting layers where approximation error is highest, with theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness demonstrated on complex scientific problems.

title: Guidance Breaks the Fitted Operator: A Terminal-Fitted Repair for Classifier-Free Guidance
summary: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) is the standard way to strengthen class-conditioning in diffusion and flow-matching samplers, yet at large guidance it oversaturates and destabilizes, symptoms practitioners suppress with more steps or limited-interval schedules. We analyze CFG through an asymptotic-preserving, numerical-analysis lens. Building on a recent result that the deterministic DDIM step is the unique fitted operator for the unguided terminal layer, exact on the final small-sigma stretch of sampling, we show that guidance re-stiffens exactly the discriminative subspace to an anomalous exponent 1+w. DDIM is therefore no longer fitted there, and on coarse meshes its guided residual diverges as sigma_min goes to zero. We prove a guided clock barrier with three ordered step-size thresholds, and read one-step oversaturation as its endpoint: a solver artifact on the calibration model rather than the continuous guided law. The same analysis yields a one-coefficient, zero-extra-NFE repair: replace CFG's w(r-1) by r^(1+w)-r on the guidance direction. On the calibration model's discriminative crossover, this removes CFG's sigma_min-divergent blow-up and is first-order accurate against the exact guided flow as sigma_min goes to zero. On learned CIFAR-10 checkpoints, and as a cross-domain smoke test on Stable Diffusion 1.5 DDIM, it acts as a high-guidance stabilizer at no extra cost rather than a universal quality knob: it cuts residual amplification and saturation, gives 9/9 point-FID wins over CFG on the tested grid, and preserves classifier-proxy target accuracy in the hard-cell blocks. We report the limits alongside: it is not a universal image-quality win, and against a dense vanilla-CFG reference it is not a uniformly better integrator of that field.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to fix classifier-free guidance (CFG) instability at high guidance weights by replacing the CFG formulation with r^(1+w)-r, which eliminates sigma_min divergence and stabilizes sampling.
AI.motivation: CFG suffers from oversaturation and instability at large guidance weights, forcing practitioners to use more steps or limited-interval schedules. The paper aims to understand and fix this fundamental instability in guided diffusion sampling.
AI.method: The authors analyze CFG through an asymptotic-preserving numerical analysis lens. They show that guidance restiffens the discriminative subspace to exponent 1+w, making DDIM no longer fitted. They prove a guided clock barrier with three step-size thresholds and propose replacing CFG's w(r-1) with r^(1+w)-r on the guidance direction.
AI.result: The proposed method removes CFG's sigma_min-divergent blow-up and is first-order accurate against exact guided flow. On CIFAR-10 checkpoints and Stable Diffusion 1.5 DDIM, it cuts residual amplification and saturation, achieves 9/9 point-FID wins over CFG, and preserves classifier-proxy target accuracy in hard-cell blocks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed repair acts as a high-guidance stabilizer at no extra computational cost, though it's not a universal image-quality win nor uniformly better integrator of the vanilla-CFG field. The work provides fundamental understanding and practical fix for CFG instability.

title: PeTeR: Post-Training Robustification of Probabilistic Circuits
summary: Probabilistic circuits (PCs) can model complex joint distributions while supporting exact and efficient computation of many inference queries. However, standard likelihood-based PC learning is vulnerable to overfitting and fragile generalization when confronted with data noise, small sample sizes, or distribution shifts. This can be mitigated using distributionally-robust optimization which consider worst-case distributions within a Wasserstein ball of the empirical distribution, but current methods are limited to training a model from scratch in this framework. Instead, we propose PeTeR: a novel, data-free post-training framework designed to robustify pre-trained PCs against distribution shifts without retraining from scratch. Empirical evaluations across multiple density estimation benchmarks demonstrate that PeTeR effectively robustifies baseline models against both random and adversarial perturbations, achieving competitive or superior performance to data-dependent robust learning baselines.

AI.tldr: PeTeR: A data-free post-training framework that robustifies pre-trained probabilistic circuits against distribution shifts without retraining from scratch.
AI.motivation: Standard probabilistic circuit learning is vulnerable to overfitting and fragile generalization with noisy data, small samples, or distribution shifts. While distributionally-robust optimization helps, existing methods require training from scratch, which is computationally expensive.
AI.method: PeTeR (Post-training robustification framework) is a novel data-free approach that robustifies pre-trained PCs against distribution shifts without requiring retraining from scratch. It considers worst-case distributions within a Wasserstein ball of the empirical distribution but operates post-training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple density estimation benchmarks show PeTeR effectively robustifies baseline models against both random and adversarial perturbations, achieving competitive or superior performance to data-dependent robust learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: PeTeR provides an effective post-training solution for robustifying probabilistic circuits against distribution shifts, offering advantages over traditional retraining approaches while maintaining or improving performance.

title: Max Out GRPO Signal: Adaptive Trace Prefix Control for Hard Reasoning Problems
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) stalls on a model's hardest problems: when no rollout in a group succeeds, the group-relative advantages vanish and the problem contributes no gradient, wasting the frontier examples we most want to learn from. Prepending a correct prefix of a reference solution raises the success rate, making prefix length a continuous knob on difficulty. Concurrent methods set the knob once; AdaPrefix-GRPO turns it into a feedback controller: throughout training it adjusts how much of the solution each problem gets, holding its success rate near 50%, where GRPO's gradient signal is largest, then withdraws the assistance entirely, so the deployed model solves problems unaided. On hard math, at matched training FLOPs, it more than doubles GRPO's accuracy on held-out problems from the training distribution for a 0.6B model (2.1x), with 1.6x on Qwen3-1.7B and 1.7x on AIME, while roughly halving trace length. The method is implemented in data preparation plus a loss mask on prefix tokens; the trainer is otherwise stock. The smaller the model, the larger the gain.

AI.tldr: AdaPrefix-GRPO adaptively adjusts solution prefixes during training to maintain optimal difficulty and gradient signals, dramatically improving performance on hard problems while eventually training models to solve problems unaided.
AI.motivation: Standard Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) fails on the hardest problems where no rollout succeeds, causing group-relative advantages to vanish and resulting in no learning gradient from the most challenging examples.
AI.method: The method uses adaptive prefixing: throughout training it adjusts how much of a reference solution each problem receives, maintaining a near-50% success rate where GRPO's gradient signal is largest, then completely withdraws the assistance. This is implemented via data preparation plus a loss mask on prefix tokens while keeping the trainer otherwise unchanged.
AI.result: On hard math problems, at matched training FLOPs, AdaPrefix-GRPO more than doubles GRPO's accuracy for a 0.6B model (2.1x improvement), with 1.6x improvement on Qwen3-1.7B and 1.7x on AIME, while roughly halving trace length. Smaller models show larger gains.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive difficulty control through prefix adjustment significantly improves learning efficiency on challenging problems, allowing models to eventually solve problems without assistance while maximizing gradient signal during training.

title: How Data Shapes RoPE Frequency Usage: From Positional Scale Matching to Length Generalization
summary: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) provide transformers with a fixed grid of positional frequencies, yet trained models use these frequencies highly non-uniformly. We study what determines this frequency usage and propose a data-centered explanation: RoPE frequencies are selected to match the relative-distance structure of the training data. Viewing each frequency as a positional lens, we formalize a field-resolution tradeoff and show that, for a data-induced dependency profile of width $W$, the optimal frequency scales as $1/W$. This frequency-matching principle explains controlled observations on synthetic and text-based data, and suggests that the mid-low frequency bands observed in language models arise from the multi-scale dependency structure of natural language. We further connect frequency selection to position-interpolation-based length generalization: scaling frequencies down expands the effective field while reducing resolution. This helps when longer-context dependencies are approximate dilations of those seen during training, but can fail when relevant dependencies do not scale with context length. Empirically, we show that natural language exhibits approximate self-similarity across positional scales, explaining why test-time frequency scaling can support long-context generalization. Overall, our results identify a data-driven mechanism behind emergent RoPE frequency usage and show that long-context generalization depends on two forms of scale matching: between learned frequencies and training-time dependencies, and between frequency scaling and how those dependencies extend to longer contexts.

AI.tldr: RoPE frequencies are selected to match the positional dependency structure of training data, explaining the non-uniform frequency usage in transformers. The study shows optimal frequency scales inversely with dependency width, and connects this to length generalization through frequency scaling and data self-similarity.
AI.motivation: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) in transformers use frequencies non-uniformly despite having a fixed grid. The paper aims to understand what determines this frequency selection and how it relates to training data structure and long-context generalization.
AI.method: The study formalizes a field-resolution tradeoff where each frequency acts as a positional lens. It proposes that RoPE frequencies are selected to match the relative-distance structure of training data, with optimal frequency scaling inversely with dependency width. The approach is validated through controlled observations on synthetic and text-based data, and connects frequency selection to position-interpolation-based length generalization.
AI.result: Results show that the frequency-matching principle explains observed frequency usage patterns, with mid-low frequency bands in language models arising from natural language's multi-scale dependency structure. The study demonstrates that natural language exhibits approximate self-similarity across positional scales, explaining why test-time frequency scaling supports long-context generalization. Empirical evidence shows frequency scaling works when longer-context dependencies are approximate dilations of training dependencies.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies a data-driven mechanism behind emergent RoPE frequency usage, showing that long-context generalization depends on two forms of scale matching: (1) between learned frequencies and training-time dependencies, and (2) between frequency scaling and how those dependencies extend to longer contexts. This provides a principled explanation for RoPE frequency selection and its relationship to length generalization.

title: Neural Operator-enabled Topology-informed Evolutionary Strategy for PDE-Constrained Optimization
summary: The inverse design of physical systems governed by partial differential equations is computationally demanding due to the high dimensionality and non-convexity of design spaces. Generative models for inverse design often lack robustness and transferability, whereas evolutionary strategies are robust but struggle in high-dimensional spaces. This paper introduces a Neural Operator-enabled Topology-informed Evolutionary Strategy (NOTES) that integrates dimensionality reduction, representation learning, and evolutionary optimization for efficient and transferable inverse design. NOTES couples a DeepONet-based neural operator with the Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES) to perform global optimization in a compact latent space that encodes topology-aware priors while discovering high-performance designs for unseen operating conditions. Applied to nanophotonic beam-deflector inverse design governed by Maxwell's equations, NOTES reduces the design dimensionality from 256 to 25 and consistently achieves over 95 percent efficiency, outperforming CMA-ES, topology optimization, and other baselines. Applied to structural optimization, NOTES discovers designs that achieve compliance down to 246. By decoupling topology learning of a DeepONet from the governing physics in a PDE solver, NOTES provides a flexible and transferable framework for the inverse design of physical systems.

AI.tldr: NOTES is a novel inverse design framework that combines neural operators with evolutionary strategies for efficient and transferable optimization of physical systems governed by PDEs.
AI.motivation: Traditional inverse design methods for PDE-governed systems face challenges: generative models lack robustness and transferability, while evolutionary strategies struggle in high-dimensional design spaces. There's a need for efficient, robust, and transferable inverse design frameworks.
AI.method: NOTES integrates DeepONet-based neural operators with Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES). It performs optimization in a compact latent space that encodes topology-aware priors, decoupling topology learning from physics simulation. The approach combines dimensionality reduction, representation learning, and evolutionary optimization.
AI.result: In nanophotonic beam-deflector design (Maxwell's equations), NOTES reduced dimensionality from 256 to 25 and consistently achieved over 95% efficiency, outperforming CMA-ES, topology optimization, and other baselines. In structural optimization, it discovered designs achieving compliance down to 246.
AI.conclusion: NOTES provides a flexible, transferable framework for inverse design that outperforms existing methods by efficiently navigating high-dimensional design spaces while maintaining robustness and discovering high-performance designs for unseen operating conditions.

title: ECGLight: Compute-Light Framework For Paper ECG Digitization and Myocardial Infarction Screening
summary: Electrocardiography (ECG) is one of the most widely used tests for diagnosing cardiovascular disease. Yet several remote clinics still utilize paper ECG printouts for their analysis due to limited connectivity and computational capacity. As a result, vast numbers of physical ECGs obtained in remote areas still remain incapable of being accessed by contemporary artificial-intelligence (AI)-based decision support as they require high computational resources or strong high-speed internet connectivity. This causes several cases where conditions like acute coronary occlusion (ACS) is overlooked and reperfusion therapy delayed. Although prior work has tackled digitization and diagnosis separately, and utilized advanced AI models for them, there still remains a lack of a compute-light, on-device framework that reconstructs paper ECGs at high fidelity, while accurately supporting multiple clinically relevant endpoints. We address this need with an end-to-end lightweight on-device digitization-to-diagnosis pipeline that converts a smartphone photo or scan of a paper ECG into a calibrated 12-lead signal and screens for Myocardial Infarction (MI) pathologies, with SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to support interpretability. Trained and evaluated on 21,799 ECGs from the PTB-XL dataset and further validated on hospital-acquired ECG-Matrix dataset, the complete system runs in <30 s per ECG on CPU-only resources, achieving 95.51% accuracy (F1 = 0.9519) for MI detection on PTB-XL and 88.89% accuracy (F1 = 0.8862) for OMI detection on ECG-Matrix. This work showcases that legacy paper records can be reliably democratized in any part of the world, providing a scalable decision support when digital ECG export, connectivity, or high-end compute are unavailable

AI.tldr: Lightweight on-device AI system converts smartphone photos of paper ECGs into digitized signals and screens for myocardial infarction with high accuracy, enabling access to AI-based diagnosis in resource-limited settings.
AI.motivation: Many remote clinics use paper ECG printouts due to limited connectivity and computational resources, making them inaccessible to contemporary AI-based decision support systems. This leads to overlooked acute coronary conditions and delayed reperfusion therapy, creating a need for compute-light, on-device solutions that can digitize and analyze paper ECGs locally without requiring high-speed internet or advanced computational infrastructure.
AI.method: End-to-end lightweight on-device digitization-to-diagnosis pipeline that converts smartphone photos/scans of paper ECGs into calibrated 12-lead signals and screens for Myocardial Infarction pathologies. Uses SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) for interpretability. Trained on 21,799 ECGs from PTB-XL dataset and validated on hospital-acquired ECG-Matrix dataset, running in <30 seconds per ECG on CPU-only resources.
AI.result: Achieved 95.51% accuracy (F1 = 0.9519) for MI detection on PTB-XL dataset and 88.89% accuracy (F1 = 0.8862) for OMI detection on ECG-Matrix dataset. The complete system runs efficiently on CPU-only resources, demonstrating practical feasibility for resource-limited settings.
AI.conclusion: Legacy paper ECG records can be reliably democratized worldwide through lightweight on-device AI systems, providing scalable decision support where digital ECG export, connectivity, or high-end computing resources are unavailable, potentially improving cardiovascular disease diagnosis in remote and underserved areas.

title: The Key to Going Linear: Analysis-Driven Transformer Linearization
summary: The quadratic cost of causal self-attention severely bottlenecks long-context transformer inference. While numerous post hoc linearization pipelines exist, it is difficult to identify which components preserve model quality. This work isolates the effect of state update design in a strict frozen-backbone regime. We show that softmax relies on key-dependent, rank-1 orthogonal projections, elucidating why delta-style networks outperform purely gated accumulation. We identify a potential source of approximation errors and introduce structural interventions, specifically sink tokens, short convolutions, and fixed-budget cache routing, which reduces the remaining gap. We scale this linearization approach across LLaMA and Qwen models up to 32B parameters, outperforming prior post hoc baselines on MMLU and matching the long-context retrieval of complex adaptive-caching frameworks.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes why delta-style networks outperform gated accumulation in linearized transformers, identifies approximation error sources, and introduces structural interventions to close the quality gap while enabling efficient long-context inference.
AI.motivation: The quadratic cost of causal self-attention severely bottlenecks long-context transformer inference, and while post hoc linearization pipelines exist, it's unclear which components preserve model quality.
AI.method: Isolates the effect of state update design in a strict frozen-backbone regime, analyzes softmax's reliance on key-dependent rank-1 orthogonal projections, identifies approximation error sources, and introduces structural interventions: sink tokens, short convolutions, and fixed-budget cache routing.
AI.result: Outperforms prior post hoc baselines on MMLU and matches the long-context retrieval performance of complex adaptive-caching frameworks when scaling the approach across LLaMA and Qwen models up to 32B parameters.
AI.conclusion: The structural interventions effectively reduce the remaining quality gap in linearized transformers, enabling efficient long-context inference while maintaining competitive performance with complex adaptive systems.
